title: New Haven, Connecticut

infobox.value: [South Central Connecticut](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut")
[Tweed New Haven Airport](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Airport "Tweed New Haven Airport")

is a city in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"). It is located on [New Haven Harbor](/wiki/New_Haven_Harbor "New Haven Harbor") on the northern shore of [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"). With a population of 134,023 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) it is the [third-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Connecticut "List of municipalities in Connecticut") in Connecticut and the largest in the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut"), with the [Greater New Haven](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven") metropolitan area having an estimated 577,000 residents.[[4]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-4)

**New Haven** is a city in the [U.S. state](/wiki/U.S._state "U.S. state") of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"). It is located on [New Haven Harbor](/wiki/New_Haven_Harbor "New Haven Harbor") on the northern shore of [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"). With a population of 134,023 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) it is the [third-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_Connecticut "List of municipalities in Connecticut") in Connecticut and the largest in the [South Central Connecticut Planning Region](/wiki/South_Central_Connecticut_Planning_Region,_Connecticut "South Central Connecticut Planning Region, Connecticut"), with the [Greater New Haven](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven") metropolitan area having an estimated 577,000 residents.[[4]](#cite_note-PopEstCBSA-4)

New Haven was one of the first [planned cities](/wiki/Planned_community "Planned community") in the U.S.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) A year after its founding by English [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") in 1638, eight streets were laid out in a three-by-three [grid](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan"), creating the "Nine Square Plan".[[8]](#cite_note-nhtowngreen-8) The central common block is [the New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), a 16-acre (6 ha) square at the center of [Downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven"). The Green is now a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), and the "Nine Square Plan" is recognized by the [American Planning Association](/wiki/American_Planning_Association "American Planning Association") as a National Planning Landmark.[[9]](#cite_note-National_Planning_Landmark_Award-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

New Haven is the home of [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), New Haven's biggest taxpayer and employer,[[11]](#cite_note-11) and an integral part of the city's economy. Health care, professional and financial services, and retail trade also contribute to the city's economic activity.

The city served as co-capital of Connecticut from 1701 to 1873, when sole governance was transferred to the more centrally located city of [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"). New Haven has since billed itself as the "Cultural Capital of Connecticut" for its many theaters, museums, and music venues.[[12]](#cite_note-12) New Haven had the first public tree planting program in the U.S., producing a canopy of mature trees (including some large [elms](/wiki/Elm "Elm")) that gave the city the nickname "The Elm City".[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Pre-colonial foundation as an independent colony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pre-colonial foundation as an independent colony")]

Before Europeans arrived, the New Haven area was the home of the [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac "Quinnipiac") tribe of Native Americans, who lived in villages around the harbor and sustained an economy of local fisheries and the farming of maize. The area was briefly visited by Dutch explorer [Adriaen Block](/wiki/Adriaen_Block "Adriaen Block") in 1614. Dutch traders set up a small trading system of [beaver](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver") pelts with the local inhabitants, but trade was sporadic and the Dutch did not settle permanently in the area.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Atwater1881_p10_Map_New_Haven_in_1641.jpg/250px-Atwater1881_p10_Map_New_Haven_in_1641.jpg)](/wiki/File:Atwater1881_p10_Map_New_Haven_in_1641.jpg)

The 1638 nine-square plan, with the extant [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green") at its center, continues to define New Haven's downtown.

In 1637, a small party of Puritans did a reconnaissance of the New Haven harbor area and wintered over. In April 1638, the main party of five hundred Puritans, who had left the [Massachusetts Bay Colony](/wiki/Massachusetts_Bay_Colony "Massachusetts Bay Colony") under the leadership of Reverend [John Davenport](/wiki/John_Davenport_(Puritan) "John Davenport (Puritan)") and London merchant [Theophilus Eaton](/wiki/Theophilus_Eaton "Theophilus Eaton"), sailed into the harbor. It was their hope to set up a theological community with the government more closely linked to the church than that in Massachusetts, and to exploit the area's potential as a port. The Quinnipiacs, who were under attack by neighboring [Pequots](/wiki/Pequot_people "Pequot people"), sold their land to the settlers in return for a pledge of protection.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Government of New Haven Colony](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Government_of_New_Haven_Colony "s:en:Government of New Haven Colony")**

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/a1/Theophilus_Eaton_house_in_New_Haven.png/250px-Theophilus_Eaton_house_in_New_Haven.png)](/wiki/File:Theophilus_Eaton_house_in_New_Haven.png)

House of New Haven Founder [Theophilus Eaton](/wiki/Theophilus_Eaton "Theophilus Eaton") as it stood at Orange and Elm streets in the 17th century

By 1640, "Quinnipiac's" [theocratic](/wiki/Theocracy "Theocracy") government and nine-square grid plan were in place, and the [town](/wiki/New_England_town "New England town") was renamed New Haven, with '[haven](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/haven#Etymology "wiktionary:haven")' meaning harbor or port. However, the area to the north remained Quinnipiac until 1678, when it was renamed [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"). The settlement became the headquarters of the [New Haven Colony](/wiki/New_Haven_Colony "New Haven Colony"), distinct from the [Connecticut Colony](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony") previously established to the north centering on [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"). Reflecting its theocratic roots, the New Haven Colony forbade the establishment of other churches, whereas the Connecticut Colony permitted them.

Economic disaster struck New Haven in 1646, when the town sent its first fully loaded ship of local goods (the "Great Shippe") back to England. It never reached its destination, and its disappearance hindered New Haven's development as compared to the rising trade powers of [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") (modern day New York City).

In 1660, Colony founder John Davenport's wishes were fulfilled, and [Hopkins School](/wiki/Hopkins_School "Hopkins School") was founded in New Haven with money from the estate of [Edward Hopkins](/wiki/Edward_Hopkins "Edward Hopkins").

In 1661, the [Regicides](/wiki/Regicide_of_Charles_I_of_England "Regicide of Charles I of England") who had signed the death warrant of [Charles I of England](/wiki/Charles_I_of_England "Charles I of England") were pursued by [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"). Two of them, Colonel [Edward Whalley](/wiki/Edward_Whalley "Edward Whalley") and Colonel [William Goffe](/wiki/William_Goffe "William Goffe"), fled to New Haven for refuge. Davenport arranged for them to hide in the [West Rock](/wiki/West_Rock "West Rock") hills northwest of the town. A third judge, [John Dixwell](/wiki/John_Dixwell "John Dixwell"), later joined the others. None of the three were ever returned to England for trial; Dixwell died of old age in New Haven, the others likewise elsewhere in New England.

### As part of the Connecticut Colony

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: As part of the Connecticut Colony")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/New_Haven_1786.jpeg/250px-New_Haven_1786.jpeg)](/wiki/File:New_Haven_1786.jpeg)

New Haven as it appeared in a 1786 engraving

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/41/Second_meeting_house%2C_new_haven%2C_ct.png/250px-Second_meeting_house%2C_new_haven%2C_ct.png)](/wiki/File:Second_meeting_house,_new_haven,_ct.png)

Second meeting house on the New Haven Green, as it stood from 1670 to 1757

In 1664, New Haven became part of the Connecticut Colony when the two colonies were merged under political pressure from England. Seeking to establish a new theocracy elsewhere, some members of the New Haven Colony went on to establish [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey").

New Haven was made co-capital of Connecticut in 1701, a status it retained until 1873.

In 1716, the Collegiate School relocated from [Old Saybrook](/wiki/Old_Saybrook,_Connecticut "Old Saybrook, Connecticut") to New Haven, establishing it as a center of learning. In 1718, in response to a large donation from [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") merchant [Elihu Yale](/wiki/Elihu_Yale "Elihu Yale"), former Governor of [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras"), the Collegiate School was renamed [Yale College](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

For over a century, New Haven citizens had fought in the colonial militia alongside regular British forces, including the [French and Indian War](/wiki/French_and_Indian_War "French and Indian War"). As the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") approached, General [David Wooster](/wiki/David_Wooster "David Wooster") and other influential residents hoped that the conflict with the [British parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain") could be resolved short of rebellion. On April 23, 1775, Captain [Benedict Arnold](/wiki/Benedict_Arnold "Benedict Arnold") commanded the Second Company, [Governor's Foot Guard](/wiki/Governor%27s_Foot_Guard "Governor's Foot Guard"), of New Haven to break into the powder house to arm themselves for a three-day march to [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts"), an event still celebrated in New Haven as [Powder House Day](/wiki/Powder_House_Day "Powder House Day"). Other New Haven militia members were on hand to escort [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") from his overnight stay in New Haven on his way to Cambridge. Contemporary reports, from both sides, remark on the New Haven volunteers' professional military bearing, including uniforms.

On July 5, 1779, 2,600 loyalists and British regulars under General [William Tryon](/wiki/William_Tryon "William Tryon"), governor of New York, landed in New Haven Harbor and [raided](/wiki/Tryon%27s_raid "Tryon's raid") the town of 3,500. A militia of Yale students had been preparing for battle, and former Yale president and [Yale Divinity School](/wiki/Yale_Divinity_School "Yale Divinity School") professor [Naphtali Daggett](/wiki/Naphtali_Daggett "Naphtali Daggett") rode out to confront the Redcoats. Yale president Ezra Stiles recounted in his diary that while he moved furniture in anticipation of battle, he still couldn't quite believe the revolution had begun.[[16]](#cite_note-16) New Haven was not torched as the invaders did with [Danbury](/wiki/Danbury,_Connecticut "Danbury, Connecticut") in 1777, or [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield,_Connecticut "Fairfield, Connecticut") and [Norwalk](/wiki/Norwalk,_Connecticut "Norwalk, Connecticut") a week after the New Haven raid, preserving many of the town's colonial features.

### Post-colonial period and industrialization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Post-colonial period and industrialization")]

New Haven was incorporated as a city in 1784, and [Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman"), one of the signers of the [Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") and author of the "[Connecticut Compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise")", became the new city's first mayor.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Harbor_and_long_Wharf%2C_from_Depot_Tower%2C_New_Haven%2C_by_Whitney%2C_Beckwith_%26_Paradice.png/250px-Harbor_and_long_Wharf%2C_from_Depot_Tower%2C_New_Haven%2C_by_Whitney%2C_Beckwith_%26_Paradice.png)](/wiki/File:Harbor_and_long_Wharf,_from_Depot_Tower,_New_Haven,_by_Whitney,_Beckwith_%26_Paradice.png)

New Haven's harbor and long wharf as seen from Depot Tower, c. 1849

The city struck fortune in the late 18th century with the inventions and industrial activity of [Eli Whitney](/wiki/Eli_Whitney "Eli Whitney"), a Yale graduate who remained in New Haven to develop the [cotton gin](/wiki/Milling_machine "Milling machine") and establish a gun-manufacturing factory in the northern part of the city near the [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut") town line. That area is still known as [Whitneyville](/wiki/Whitneyville,_Connecticut "Whitneyville, Connecticut"), and the main road through both towns is known as Whitney Avenue. The factory is now the [Eli Whitney Museum](/wiki/Eli_Whitney_Museum "Eli Whitney Museum"), which has a particular emphasis on activities for children and exhibits pertaining to the [A. C. Gilbert Company](/wiki/A._C._Gilbert_Company "A. C. Gilbert Company"). His factory, along with that of [Simeon North](/wiki/Simeon_North "Simeon North"), and the lively clock-making and brass hardware sectors, contributed to making early Connecticut a powerful manufacturing economy; so many arms manufacturers sprang up that the state became known as "The Arsenal of America". It was in Whitney's gun-manufacturing plant that [Samuel Colt](/wiki/Samuel_Colt "Samuel Colt") invented the [automatic revolver](/wiki/Revolver "Revolver") in 1836. Many other talented machinists and firearms designers would go on to found successful firearms manufacturing companies in New Haven, including [Oliver Winchester](/wiki/Oliver_Winchester "Oliver Winchester") and [O.F. Mossberg & Sons](/wiki/O.F._Mossberg_%26_Sons "O.F. Mossberg & Sons").

The [Farmington Canal](/wiki/Farmington_Canal "Farmington Canal"), created in the early 19th century, was a short-lived transporter of goods into the interior regions of Connecticut and Massachusetts, and ran from New Haven to [Northampton, Massachusetts](/wiki/Northampton,_Massachusetts "Northampton, Massachusetts").

New Haven was to be the site of the first college for African Americans in the United States, but the plan was obstructed by efforts led by Yale [Law School](/wiki/Law_School "Law School") founder and former New Haven Mayor [David Daggett](/wiki/David_Daggett "David Daggett"), who went on to serve as a U.S. Senator and judge on Connecticut's highest court. Daggett denigrated African Americans, denied they were citizens, and presided over the trial of a woman persecuted for trying to admit an African American girl to her boarding school and, having that effort blocked, running a boarding school for African American girls.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

New Haven was home to one of the important early events in the burgeoning [anti-slavery movement](/wiki/Antislavery_Movement_In_America "Antislavery Movement In America") when, in 1839, the trial of mutineering [Mende tribesmen](/wiki/Mende_people "Mende people") being transported as slaves on the Spanish [slaveship](/wiki/Slaveship "Slaveship") *[Amistad](/wiki/La_Amistad "La Amistad")* was held in [New Haven's United States District Court](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Connecticut "United States District Court for the District of Connecticut").[[19]](#cite_note-WDL-19) There is a [statue](/wiki/Amistad_memorial_(New_Haven) "Amistad memorial (New Haven)") of [Joseph Cinqué](/wiki/Joseph_Cinqu%C3%A9 "Joseph Cinqué"), the informal leader of the slaves, beside City Hall. See "Museums" below for more information. [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") delivered a speech on slavery in New Haven in 1860,[[20]](#cite_note-20) shortly before he secured the [Republican nomination for President](/wiki/1860_Republican_National_Convention "1860 Republican National Convention").

The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") boosted the local economy with wartime purchases of industrial goods, including that of the [New Haven Arms Company](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company#Predecessors "Winchester Repeating Arms Company"), which would later become the [Winchester Repeating Arms Company](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company "Winchester Repeating Arms Company"). (Winchester would continue to produce arms in New Haven until 2006, and many of the buildings that were a part of the Winchester plant are now a part of the [Winchester Repeating Arms Company Historic District](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company_Historic_District "Winchester Repeating Arms Company Historic District")).[[21]](#cite_note-21) After the war, population grew and doubled by the start of the 20th century, most notably due to the influx of immigrants from southern Europe, particularly Italy. Today, roughly half the populations of East Haven, West Haven, and North Haven are [Italian-American](/wiki/Italian-American "Italian-American"). Jewish immigration to New Haven has left an enduring mark on the city. Westville was the center of [Jewish](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") life in New Haven, though today many have fanned out to suburban communities such as Woodbridge and Cheshire. [Lowell House](/wiki/Lowell_House_(New_Haven,_Connecticut) "Lowell House (New Haven, Connecticut)"), the city's first [settlement](/wiki/Settlement_movement "Settlement movement"), opened in 1900.[[22]](#cite_note-Stokes-1946-22)

### Post-industrial era and urban redevelopment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-industrial era and urban redevelopment")]

New Haven's expansion continued during the two World Wars, with most new inhabitants being African Americans from the [American South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"), and [Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_people "Puerto Rican people"). The city reached its peak population after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The area of New Haven is only 17 square miles (44 km2), encouraging further development of new housing after 1950 in adjacent, suburban towns. Moreover, as in other U.S. cities in the [1950s](/wiki/United_States_in_the_1950s "United States in the 1950s"), New Haven began to suffer [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") of middle-class workers. One author suggested that aggressive [redlining](/wiki/Redlining "Redlining") and rezoning made it difficult for residents to obtain financing for older, deteriorating urban housing stock, thereby condemning such structures to deterioration.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[*[additional citation(s) needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1954, then-mayor [Richard C. Lee](/wiki/Richard_C._Lee "Richard C. Lee") began some of the earliest major [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") projects in the United States. Certain sections of [downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven") were redeveloped to include museums, new office towers, a hotel, and large shopping complexes.[[24]](#cite_note-time.com-24) Other parts of the city, particularly the Wooster Square and [Fair Haven](/wiki/Fair_Haven_(New_Haven) "Fair Haven (New Haven)") neighborhoods were affected by the construction of [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Connecticut "Interstate 95 in Connecticut") along the Long Wharf section, [Interstate 91](/wiki/Interstate_91 "Interstate 91"), and the [Oak Street Connector](/wiki/Oak_Street_Connector "Oak Street Connector"). The Oak Street Connector ([Route 34](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_34 "Connecticut Route 34")), running between Interstate 95, downtown, and [The Hill](/wiki/The_Hill_(New_Haven) "The Hill (New Haven)") neighborhood, was originally intended as a highway to the city's western suburbs but was only completed as a highway to the downtown area, with the area to the west becoming a boulevard (See "Redevelopment" below).

In 1970, a [series of criminal prosecutions](/wiki/New_Haven_Black_Panther_trials "New Haven Black Panther trials") against various members of the [Black Panther Party](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") took place in New Haven, inciting mass protests on the New Haven Green involving twelve thousand demonstrators and many well-known [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left") political activists. (See "Political Culture" below for more information). From the 1960s through the early 1990s, some areas of New Haven continued to decline both economically and in terms of population despite attempts to resurrect certain neighborhoods through renewal projects. In conjunction with its declining population, New Haven experienced a steep rise in its [crime rate](/wiki/Crime_rate "Crime rate").

Since approximately 2000, many parts of downtown New Haven have been revitalized with new restaurants, nightlife, and small retail stores. In particular, the area surrounding the New Haven Green has experienced an influx of apartments and condominiums. In addition, two new supermarkets opened to serve downtown's growing residential population: a Stop and Shop opened just west of downtown, while Elm City Market, located one block from the Green, opened in 2011.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The recent turnaround of downtown New Haven has received positive press from various periodicals.[[26]](#cite_note-Charles-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Major projects include the current construction of a new campus for Gateway Community College downtown, and also a 32-story, 500-unit apartment/retail building called [360 State Street](/wiki/360_State_Street "360 State Street"). The 360 State Street project is now occupied and is the largest residential building in Connecticut.[[29]](#cite_note-29) A new boathouse and dock is planned for New Haven Harbor, and the linear park [Farmington Canal Trail](/wiki/Farmington_Canal_Trail "Farmington Canal Trail") is set to extend into downtown New Haven within the coming year.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Additionally, foundation and ramp work to widen I-95 to create a new harbor crossing for New Haven, with an extradosed bridge to replace the 1950s-era [Q Bridge](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_Memorial_Bridge_(Connecticut) "Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge (Connecticut)"), has begun.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The city still hopes to redevelop the site of the [New Haven Coliseum](/wiki/New_Haven_Coliseum "New Haven Coliseum"), which was demolished in 2007.

In April 2009, the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") agreed to [hear a suit](/wiki/Ricci_v._DeStefano "Ricci v. DeStefano") over [reverse discrimination](/wiki/Reverse_discrimination "Reverse discrimination") brought by 18 white firefighters against the city. The suit involved the 2003 promotion test for the New Haven Fire Department. After the tests were scored, no [black](/wiki/African_American "African American") firefighters scored high enough to qualify for consideration for promotion, so the city announced that no one would be promoted. In the subsequent *[Ricci v. DeStefano](/wiki/Ricci_v._DeStefano "Ricci v. DeStefano")* decision the court found 5–4 that New Haven's decision to ignore the test results violated [Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Title_VII_of_the_Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964").[[32]](#cite_note-32) As a result, a district court subsequently ordered the city to promote 14 of the white firefighters.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2010 and 2011, state and federal funds were awarded to Connecticut (and Massachusetts) to construct the [Hartford Line](/wiki/Hartford_Line "Hartford Line"), with a southern terminus at New Haven's [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)") and a northern terminus at [Springfield's](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts") Union Station.[[34]](#cite_note-nhhsrail.com-34) According to the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House"), "This corridor [currently] has one train per day connecting communities in Connecticut and Massachusetts to the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor") and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"). The vision for this corridor is to restore the alignment to its original route via the [Knowledge Corridor](/wiki/Knowledge_Corridor "Knowledge Corridor") in [western Massachusetts](/wiki/Western_Massachusetts "Western Massachusetts"), improving trip time and increasing the population base that can be served."[[35]](#cite_note-35) Set for construction in 2013, the "Knowledge Corridor high speed intercity passenger rail" project will cost approximately $1 billion, and the ultimate northern terminus for the project is reported to be [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal") in Canada.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Train speeds between will reportedly exceed 110 miles per hour (180 km/h) and increase both cities' rail traffic exponentially.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

### Timeline of notable firsts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Timeline of notable firsts")]

See also: [Yale – New Haven Hospital § Milestones in medicine](/wiki/Yale_%E2%80%93_New_Haven_Hospital#Milestones_in_medicine "Yale – New Haven Hospital")

* 1638: New Haven becomes the first planned city in America.
* 1776: Yale student David Bushnell invents the first American [submarine](/wiki/Submarine "Submarine").
* 1787: [John Fitch](/wiki/John_Fitch_(inventor) "John Fitch (inventor)") builds the first [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat "Steamboat").
* 1836: [Samuel Colt](/wiki/Samuel_Colt "Samuel Colt") invents the [automatic revolver](/wiki/Automatic_revolver "Automatic revolver") in Whitney's [factory](/wiki/Eli_Whitney_Museum "Eli Whitney Museum").
* 1839: [Charles Goodyear](/wiki/Charles_Goodyear "Charles Goodyear") of New Haven discovers the process of [vulcanizing](/wiki/Vulcanizing "Vulcanizing") rubber in [Woburn, Massachusetts](/wiki/Woburn,_Massachusetts "Woburn, Massachusetts"), and later perfects it and patents the process in nearby [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts").[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* 1860: Philios P. Blake patents the first corkscrew.
* 1877: New Haven hosts the first [Bell](/wiki/Bell_System "Bell System") [PSTN](/wiki/PSTN "PSTN") (telephone) switch office.
* 1878–1880: The District Telephone Company of New Haven creates the world's first [telephone exchange](/wiki/Telephone_exchange "Telephone exchange") and the first [telephone directory](/wiki/Telephone_directory "Telephone directory") and installs the first public phone. The company expanded and became the Connecticut Telephone Company, then the [Southern New England Telephone Company](/wiki/Southern_New_England_Telephone_Company "Southern New England Telephone Company") (now part of [AT&T](/wiki/AT%26T_Inc. "AT&T Inc.")).[[39]](#cite_note-NPSTele-39)
* 1882: The [Knights of Columbus](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus "Knights of Columbus") are founded in New Haven. The city still serves as the world [headquarters](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus_Building_(New_Haven,_Connecticut) "Knights of Columbus Building (New Haven, Connecticut)") of the organization, which maintains a museum downtown.[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* 1892: Local confectioner George C. Smith of the Bradley Smith Candy Co. invents the first [lollipops](/wiki/Lollipop "Lollipop").[[41]](#cite_note-41)
* Late 19th century-early 20th century: The first public tree planting program takes place in New Haven, at the urging of native [James Hillhouse](/wiki/James_Hillhouse "James Hillhouse").[[42]](#cite_note-42)
* 1900: Louis Lassen, owner of [Louis' Lunch](/wiki/Louis%27_Lunch "Louis' Lunch"), is credited with inventing the [hamburger](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger"), as well as the [steak sandwich](/wiki/Steak_sandwich "Steak sandwich").[[43]](#cite_note-43)
* 1911: The [Erector Set](/wiki/Erector_Set "Erector Set"), the popular and culturally important construction toy, is invented in New Haven by [A.C. Gilbert](/wiki/Alfred_Carlton_Gilbert "Alfred Carlton Gilbert"). It was manufactured by the A. C. Gilbert Company at [Erector Square](/wiki/Erector_Square "Erector Square") from 1913 until the company's bankruptcy in 1967.[[44]](#cite_note-44)
* 1920: In competition with competing explanations, the [Frisbee](/wiki/Frisbee "Frisbee") is said to have originated on the Yale campus, based on the tin pans of the [Frisbie Pie Company](/wiki/Frisbie_Pie_Company "Frisbie Pie Company") which were tossed around by students on the [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green").[[45]](#cite_note-45)
* 1977: The first memorial to victims of [the Holocaust](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") on public land in America[[46]](#cite_note-ashes-46) stands in New Haven's [Edgewood Park](/wiki/Edgewood_Park,_New_Haven "Edgewood Park, New Haven") at the corner of Whalley and West Park avenues. It was built with funds collected from the community[[47]](#cite_note-47) and is maintained by Greater New Haven Holocaust Memory, Inc.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The ashes of victims killed and [cremated](/wiki/Cremation "Cremation") at [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz") are buried under the memorial.[[46]](#cite_note-ashes-46)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Quinnipiac_River_from_Fair_Haven.jpg/250px-Quinnipiac_River_from_Fair_Haven.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quinnipiac_River_from_Fair_Haven.jpg)

View of the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River") from [Fair Haven](/wiki/Fair_Haven_(New_Haven) "Fair Haven (New Haven)")

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 20.1 square miles (52.1 km2), of which 18.7 square miles (48.4 km2) is land and 1.4 square miles (3.7 km2), or 6.67%, is water.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

New Haven's best-known geographic features are its large, shallow harbor, and two reddish basalt [trap rock](/wiki/Trap_rock "Trap rock") ridges which rise to the northeast and northwest of the city core. These trap rocks are known respectively as [East Rock](/wiki/East_Rock "East Rock") and [West Rock](/wiki/West_Rock "West Rock"), and both serve as extensive parks. West Rock has been tunneled through to make way for the east–west passage of the [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway") (the only highway tunnel through a natural obstacle in Connecticut), and once served as the hideout of the "[Regicides](/wiki/Regicide_of_Charles_I_of_England "Regicide of Charles I of England")" (see: [Regicides Trail](/wiki/Regicides_Trail "Regicides Trail")). Most New Haveners refer to these men as "The Three Judges". East Rock features the prominent Soldiers and Sailors war monument on its peak as well as the "Great/Giant Steps" which run up the rock's cliffside.

The city is drained by three rivers; the [West](/wiki/West_River_(Connecticut) "West River (Connecticut)"), [Mill](/wiki/Mill_River_(Connecticut) "Mill River (Connecticut)"), and [Quinnipiac](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River"), named in order from west to east. The West River discharges into [West Haven](/wiki/West_Haven,_Connecticut "West Haven, Connecticut") Harbor, while the Mill and Quinnipiac rivers discharge into New Haven Harbor. Both harbors are embayments of [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound"). In addition, several smaller streams flow through the city's neighborhoods, including Wintergreen Brook, the Beaver Ponds Outlet, Wilmot Brook, Belden Brook, and Prospect Creek. Not all of these small streams have continuous flow year-round.

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Climate")]

According to the [Köppen classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), New Haven experiences a bordering a hot-summer [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfa*), bordering on a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*). The city has hot, humid summers and cool to cold winters. From May to late September, the weather is typically hot and humid, with average temperatures exceeding 80 °F (27 °C) on 70 days per year. In summer, the [Bermuda High](/wiki/Bermuda_High "Bermuda High") creates as southern flow of warm and humid air, with frequent (but brief) thundershowers. October to early December is normally mild to cool late in the season, while early spring (April) can be cool to warm. Winters are more variable, with cool to cold temperatures, mixed with warmer spells, with both rain and snow fall.

While the weather patterns that affect New Haven result from a primarily offshore direction, thus reducing the marine influence of Long Island Sound, the location on Long Island Sound often influences the weather along the coastline compared to inland. During summer heat waves, temperatures may reach 95 °F (35 °C) or higher on occasion with heat-index values of over 100 °F (38 °C). [Tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones "Tropical cyclones") have struck New Haven in the past, including 1938 Hurricane ([Long Island Express](/wiki/Long_Island_Express "Long Island Express")), [Hurricane Carol](/wiki/Hurricane_Carol "Hurricane Carol") in 1954, [Hurricane Gloria](/wiki/Hurricane_Gloria "Hurricane Gloria") in 1985.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) The [hardiness zone](/wiki/USDA_hardiness_zone "USDA hardiness zone") is 7a.

### Streetscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Streetscape")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Yale_University_08.jpg/250px-Yale_University_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yale_University_08.jpg)

The city from the south with [The Hill](/wiki/The_Hill,_New_Haven "The Hill, New Haven") in the foreground. [East Rock](/wiki/East_Rock,_New_Haven "East Rock, New Haven") is visible in the background.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Large_American_Elm_Tree%2C_New_Haven%2C_CT_-_June_10%2C_2017.jpg/250px-Large_American_Elm_Tree%2C_New_Haven%2C_CT_-_June_10%2C_2017.jpg)](/wiki/File:Large_American_Elm_Tree,_New_Haven,_CT_-_June_10,_2017.jpg)

[American elm](/wiki/Ulmus_americana "Ulmus americana") in New Haven

New Haven has a long tradition of [urban planning](/wiki/Urban_planning "Urban planning") and a purposeful design for the city's layout.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The city could be argued to have some of the first preconceived layouts in the country.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Upon founding, New Haven was laid out in a [grid plan](/wiki/Grid_plan "Grid plan") of nine square blocks; the central square was left open, in the tradition of many New England towns, as the [city green](/wiki/Village_green "Village green") (a commons area). The city also instituted the first public tree planting program in America. As in other cities, many of the [elms](/wiki/Elm "Elm") that gave New Haven the nickname "Elm City" perished in the mid-20th century due to [Dutch elm disease](/wiki/Dutch_elm_disease "Dutch elm disease"), although many have since been replanted. The [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green") is currently home to three separate historic churches which speak to the original theocratic nature of the city.[[8]](#cite_note-nhtowngreen-8) The Green remains the social center of the city today. It was named a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") in 1970.

[Downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven"), occupied by nearly 7,000 residents, has a more residential character than most downtowns.[[59]](#cite_note-downtown-59) The downtown area provides about half of the city's jobs and half of its tax base[[59]](#cite_note-downtown-59) and in recent years has become filled with dozens of new upscale restaurants, in addition to shops and thousands of apartments and condominium units which subsequently help overall growth of the city.[[60]](#cite_note-new_development-60)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Front_Street_in_New_Haven_CT.jpg/250px-Front_Street_in_New_Haven_CT.jpg)](/wiki/File:Front_Street_in_New_Haven_CT.jpg)

The [Quinnipiac River Historic District](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River_Historic_District "Quinnipiac River Historic District"), located in the [Fair Haven](/wiki/Fair_Haven_(New_Haven) "Fair Haven (New Haven)") neighborhood, is one of dozens of listed historic districts in New Haven.

Main article: [Neighborhoods of New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/Neighborhoods_of_New_Haven,_Connecticut "Neighborhoods of New Haven, Connecticut")

The city has many distinct neighborhoods. In addition to Downtown, centered on the central business district and the [Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), are the following neighborhoods: the west central neighborhoods of [Dixwell](/wiki/Dixwell_(New_Haven) "Dixwell (New Haven)") and [Dwight](/wiki/Dwight_Street_Historic_District "Dwight Street Historic District"); the southern neighborhoods of [The Hill](/wiki/The_Hill_(New_Haven) "The Hill (New Haven)"), historic water-front [City Point](/wiki/City_Point_(New_Haven) "City Point (New Haven)") (or Oyster Point), and the harborside district of [Long Wharf](/wiki/Long_Wharf_(New_Haven) "Long Wharf (New Haven)"); the western neighborhoods of [Beaver Hills](/wiki/Beaver_Hills,_New_Haven "Beaver Hills, New Haven"), [Edgewood](/wiki/Edgewood_Park_Historic_District "Edgewood Park Historic District"), [West River](/wiki/West_River_(neighborhood) "West River (neighborhood)"), [Westville](/wiki/Westville_(New_Haven) "Westville (New Haven)"), [Amity](/wiki/Amity_(New_Haven) "Amity (New Haven)"), and [West Rock-Westhills](/wiki/West_Rock_(neighborhood) "West Rock (neighborhood)"); [East Rock](/wiki/East_Rock_(neighborhood) "East Rock (neighborhood)"), [Cedar Hill](/wiki/Cedar_Hill_(New_Haven) "Cedar Hill (New Haven)"), [Prospect Hill](/wiki/Prospect_Hill_(New_Haven) "Prospect Hill (New Haven)"), and [Newhallville](/wiki/Newhallville "Newhallville") in the northern side of town; the east central neighborhoods of [Mill River](/wiki/Mill_River_(neighborhood) "Mill River (neighborhood)") and [Wooster Square](/wiki/Wooster_Square "Wooster Square"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) an Italian-American neighborhood; [Fair Haven](/wiki/Fair_Haven_(New_Haven) "Fair Haven (New Haven)"), an immigrant community located between the Mill and Quinnipiac rivers; [Quinnipiac Meadows](/wiki/Quinnipiac_Meadows "Quinnipiac Meadows") and [Fair Haven Heights](/wiki/Fair_Haven_Heights "Fair Haven Heights") across the Quinnipiac River; and facing the eastern side of the harbor, [The Annex](/wiki/The_Annex_(New_Haven) "The Annex (New Haven)") and [East Shore](/wiki/East_Shore_(New_Haven) "East Shore (New Haven)") (or Morris Cove).[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

The [U.S. Census Bureau](/wiki/U.S._Census_Bureau "U.S. Census Bureau") reports a 2010 population of 129,779, with 47,094 households and 25,854 families within the city of New Haven. The population density was 6,859.8 inhabitants per square mile (2,648.6/km2). There were 52,941 housing units at an average density of 2,808.5 per square mile (1,084.4/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 42.6% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 35.4% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._census) "African American (U.S. census)"), 0.5% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 4.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_(U.S._Census) "Pacific Islander (U.S. Census)"), 12.9% from [other races](/wiki/Race_(United_States_Census) "Race (United States Census)"), and 3.9% from two or more races. [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_(U.S._Census) "Hispanic (U.S. Census)") or [Latino](/wiki/Latino_(U.S._Census) "Latino (U.S. Census)") residents of any race were 27.4% of the population.[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Non-Hispanic whites](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") were 31.8% of the population in 2010,[[72]](#cite_note-72) down from 69.6% in 1970.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The city's Latino population is growing rapidly. Previous influxes among ethnic groups have been African-Americans in the postwar era, and Irish, Italian and (to a lesser degree) Slavic peoples in the prewar period.

By the 2020 census, its population was 134,023; its racial and ethnic makeup was 27.61% non-Hispanic white, 30.43% Black or African American, 0.25% Native American or Alaska Native, 6.75% Asian, 0.05% Pacific Islander, 0.65% some other race, 3.61% multiracial, and 30.64% Hispanic or Latino of any race.

As of the 2010 census, of the 47,094 households, 29.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 27.5% include married couples living together, 22.9% had a female householder with no husband present, and 45.1% were non-families. 36.1% of all households were made up of individuals, and 10.5% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.40 and the average family size 3.19.[[74]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-74)[[75]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-75)

The ages of New Haven's residents were 25.4% under the age of 18, 16.4% from 18 to 24, 31.2% from 25 to 44, 16.7% from 45 to 64, and 10.2% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 29 years, which was significantly lower than the national average. There were 91.8 males per 100 females. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 87.6 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $29,604, and the median income for a family was $35,950. Median income for males was $33,605, compared with $28,424 for females. The per capita income for the city was $16,393. About 20.5% of families and 24.4% of the population were living below the poverty line, including 32.2% of those under age 18 and 17.9% of those age 65 or over.[[74]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-74)[[75]](#cite_note-autogenerated4-75)

### Other data

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Other data")]

It is estimated that 14% of New Haven residents are pedestrian commuters, ranking it number four by highest percentage in the United States. This is primarily due to New Haven's small area and the presence of [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

New Haven is noted for having the highest percentage of Italian American residents of any US city, and is noted for its [local style of pizza](/wiki/New_Haven-style_pizza "New Haven-style pizza").[[76]](#cite_note-76)

A study of the demographics of the New Haven metro area, based on age, educational attainment, and race and ethnicity, found that they were the closest of any American city to the national average.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/New_Haven_from_above%2C_2009-12-10.jpg/250px-New_Haven_from_above%2C_2009-12-10.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Haven_from_above,_2009-12-10.jpg)

The Port of New Haven

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Connecticut_-_New_Haven_-_NARA_-_23936517_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Connecticut_-_New_Haven_-_NARA_-_23936517_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Connecticut_-_New_Haven_-_NARA_-_23936517_(cropped).jpg)

Port in the 1930s

New Haven's economy originally was based in manufacturing, but the postwar period brought rapid [industrial decline](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization"); the entire Northeast was affected, and medium-sized cities with large working-class populations, like New Haven, were hit particularly hard. Simultaneously, the growth and expansion of [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") further affected the economic shift. Today, over half (56%) of the city's economy is now made up of services, in particular education and health care; Yale is the city's largest employer, followed by [Yale – New Haven Hospital](/wiki/Yale_%E2%80%93_New_Haven_Hospital "Yale – New Haven Hospital"). Other large employers include [Southern Connecticut State University](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_State_University "Southern Connecticut State University"), [Assa Abloy](/wiki/Assa_Abloy "Assa Abloy") lock manufacturing, the [Knights of Columbus](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus "Knights of Columbus") headquarters, Higher One, [Alexion Pharmaceuticals](/wiki/Alexion_Pharmaceuticals "Alexion Pharmaceuticals"), Covidien and [United Illuminating](/wiki/United_Illuminating "United Illuminating").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Clothing stores [Gant](/wiki/Gant_(retailer) "Gant (retailer)") and [Ann Taylor](/wiki/Ann_Inc. "Ann Inc.") were founded in the city.

In 2017, New Haven was ranked by a [Verizon](/wiki/Verizon_Communications "Verizon Communications") study as one of the top 10 cities in America for launching tech startups, and top two in New England.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

**Industry sectors**: Agriculture (.6%), Construction and Mining (4.9%), Manufacturing (2.9%), Transportation and Utilities (2.9%), Trade (21.7%), Finance and Real Estate (7.1%), Services (55.9%), Government (4.0%)

### Headquarters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Headquarters")]

The [Knights of Columbus](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus "Knights of Columbus"), the world's largest Catholic fraternal service organization and a [Fortune 1000](/wiki/Fortune_1000 "Fortune 1000") company, is headquartered in New Haven.[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Amphenol](/wiki/Amphenol "Amphenol"), based in Greater New Haven ([Wallingford](/wiki/Wallingford,_Connecticut "Wallingford, Connecticut")), is a Fortune 100 company.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Eight Courant 100 companies are based in Greater New Haven, with four headquartered in New Haven proper.[[81]](#cite_note-81) New Haven-based companies publicly listed include [NewAlliance Bank](/wiki/NewAlliance_Bank "NewAlliance Bank"), the second largest bank in Connecticut and fourth-largest in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") (NYSE: NAL), Higher One Holdings (NYSE: ONE), a financial services firm, [United Illuminating](/wiki/United_Illuminating "United Illuminating"), the electricity distributor for southern Connecticut (NYSE: UIL), and Transpro Inc. (AMEX: TPR). The American division of [Assa Abloy](/wiki/Assa_Abloy "Assa Abloy") (one of the world's leading manufacturers of locks) is located in the city. The [Southern New England Telephone Company](/wiki/Southern_New_England_Telephone_Company "Southern New England Telephone Company") (SNET) began operations in the city as the District Telephone Company of New Haven in 1878; the company remains headquartered in New Haven as a subsidiary of [Frontier Communications](/wiki/Frontier_Communications "Frontier Communications") and provides telephone service for all but two municipalities in Connecticut.[[82]](#cite_note-82) [SeeClickFix](/wiki/SeeClickFix "SeeClickFix") was founded and has been headquartered in the city since 2007. [Peter Paul Candy Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Candy_Manufacturing_Company "Peter Paul Candy Manufacturing Company") (a candy-making division of the [Hershey Company](/wiki/Hershey_Company "Hershey Company")) was formerly located in the city. Achillion Pharmaceuticals and Alexion Pharmaceuticals were also formerly headquartered in New Haven.

## Law and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Law and government")]

### Political structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Political structure")]

See also: [List of mayors of New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_New_Haven,_Connecticut "List of mayors of New Haven, Connecticut") and [Mayoral elections in New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/Mayoral_elections_in_New_Haven,_Connecticut "Mayoral elections in New Haven, Connecticut")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Newhavenstructure2-statue.jpg/250px-Newhavenstructure2-statue.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newhavenstructure2-statue.jpg)

Statue of Roman orator [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") at the New Haven County Courthouse

New Haven is governed via the [mayor-council system](/wiki/Mayor-council_system "Mayor-council system"). Connecticut municipalities (like those of neighboring states [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") and [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island")) provide nearly all local services (such as fire and rescue, education, snow removal, etc.), as county government has been abolished since 1960.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/New_Haven_City_Hall%2C_October_17%2C_2008.jpg/250px-New_Haven_City_Hall%2C_October_17%2C_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Haven_City_Hall,_October_17,_2008.jpg)

New Haven City Hall

[New Haven County](/wiki/New_Haven_County "New Haven County") merely refers to a grouping of towns and a judicial district, not a governmental entity. New Haven is a member of the South Central Connecticut Regional Council of Governments (SCRCOG), a regional agency created to facilitate coordination between area municipal governments and state and federal agencies, in the absence of county government.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

[Justin Elicker](/wiki/Justin_Elicker "Justin Elicker") is the mayor of New Haven. He was sworn in as the 51st mayor of New Haven on January 1, 2020.

The city council, called the Board of Alders, consists of thirty members, each elected from single-member wards.[[85]](#cite_note-85) Each of the 30 wards consists of slightly over 4,300 residents; [redistricting](/wiki/Redistricting "Redistricting") takes place every ten years.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

The city is overwhelmingly [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)"). In 2017, of the town's 83,694 voters, 66% were registered as Democrats (−4% since 2015), 4% were registered as Republicans (+1%), and 29% were unaffiliated (+3).[[87]](#cite_note-87) The board of alders is dominated by Democrats; a Republican has not served as a New Haven alder since 2011.[[88]](#cite_note-2015Alders-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

New Haven is served by the [New Haven Police Department](/wiki/New_Haven_Police_Department "New Haven Police Department"), which had 443 sworn officers in 2011.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The city is also served by the [New Haven Fire Department](/wiki/New_Haven_Fire_Department "New Haven Fire Department").

New Haven lies within [Connecticut's 3rd congressional district](/wiki/Connecticut%27s_3rd_congressional_district "Connecticut's 3rd congressional district") and has been represented by [Rosa DeLauro](/wiki/Rosa_DeLauro "Rosa DeLauro") since 1991. [Martin Looney](/wiki/Martin_Looney "Martin Looney") and [Gary Winfield](/wiki/Gary_Winfield "Gary Winfield") represent New Haven in the [Connecticut State Senate](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Senate "Connecticut State Senate"), and the city lies within six districts (numbers 92 through 97) of the [Connecticut House of Representatives](/wiki/Connecticut_House_of_Representatives "Connecticut House of Representatives").[[91]](#cite_note-91)[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The Greater New Haven area is served by the New Haven Judicial District Court and the New Haven Superior Court, both headquartered at the New Haven County Courthouse.[[93]](#cite_note-93) The [federal District Court for the District of Connecticut](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_District_of_Connecticut "United States District Court for the District of Connecticut") has a New Haven facility, the [Richard C. Lee United States Courthouse](/wiki/Richard_C._Lee_United_States_Courthouse "Richard C. Lee United States Courthouse").

### Political history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Political history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/Roger_Sherman_1721-1793_by_Ralph_Earl.jpeg/250px-Roger_Sherman_1721-1793_by_Ralph_Earl.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Roger_Sherman_1721-1793_by_Ralph_Earl.jpeg)

[Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman"), signer of [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States"), author of [Connecticut Compromise](/wiki/Connecticut_Compromise "Connecticut Compromise"), and first mayor of New Haven. Portrait by [Ralph Earl](/wiki/Ralph_Earl "Ralph Earl"), c. 1775.

See also: [List of Yale University people § Law and politics](/wiki/List_of_Yale_University_people#Law_and_politics "List of Yale University people"); and [List of people from New Haven, Connecticut § Politicians](/wiki/List_of_people_from_New_Haven,_Connecticut#Politicians "List of people from New Haven, Connecticut")

New Haven is the birthplace of former president [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush"),[[94]](#cite_note-bushbio-94) who was born when his father, former president [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush"), was living in New Haven while a student at Yale. In addition to being the site of the college educations of both Presidents Bush, as Yale students, New Haven was also the temporary home of former presidents [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft"), [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), and [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), as well as Secretary of State [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry"). President Clinton met his wife, former U.S. Secretary of State [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"), while the two were students at [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School"). Former vice presidents [John C. Calhoun](/wiki/John_C._Calhoun "John C. Calhoun") and [Dick Cheney](/wiki/Dick_Cheney "Dick Cheney") also studied in New Haven (although the latter did not graduate from Yale). Before the 2008 election, the last time there was not a person with ties to New Haven and Yale on either major party's ticket was 1968. [James Hillhouse](/wiki/James_Hillhouse "James Hillhouse"), a New Haven native, served as [President pro tempore of the United States Senate](/wiki/President_pro_tempore_of_the_United_States_Senate "President pro tempore of the United States Senate") in 1801.

New Haven voters overwhelmingly supported [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") in the [2000 election](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election"), Yale graduate [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") in [2004](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election"),[[95]](#cite_note-95) and [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") in [2008](/wiki/2008_US_presidential_election "2008 US presidential election") and 2012. In the 2008 election, [New Haven County](/wiki/New_Haven_County "New Haven County") was third among all [Connecticut counties](/wiki/Connecticut_counties "Connecticut counties") in campaign contributions, after [Fairfield](/wiki/Fairfield_County,_Connecticut "Fairfield County, Connecticut") and [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford_County "Hartford County") counties. (Connecticut, in turn, was ranked 14th among all states in total campaign contributions.)[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[97]](#cite_note-97)

New Haven was the subject of  *[Who Governs?](/wiki/Who_Governs%3F "Who Governs?") Democracy and Power in An American City*, a very influential book in [political science](/wiki/Political_science "Political science") by preeminent Yale professor [Robert A. Dahl](/wiki/Robert_A._Dahl "Robert A. Dahl"), which includes an extensive history of the city and thorough description of its politics in the 1950s. New Haven's theocratic history is also mentioned several times by [Alexis de Tocqueville](/wiki/Alexis_de_Tocqueville "Alexis de Tocqueville") in his classic volume on 19th-century American political life, *[Democracy in America](/wiki/Democracy_in_America "Democracy in America")*.[[98]](#cite_note-98) New Haven was the residence of conservative thinker [William F. Buckley, Jr.](/wiki/William_F._Buckley,_Jr. "William F. Buckley, Jr."), in 1951, when he wrote his influential *[God and Man at Yale](/wiki/God_and_Man_at_Yale "God and Man at Yale")*. [William Lee Miller](/wiki/William_Lee_Miller "William Lee Miller")'s *The Fifteenth Ward and the Great Society* (1966) similarly explores the relationship between local politics in New Haven and national political movements, focusing on [Lyndon Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_Johnson "Lyndon Johnson")'s [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") and [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal").[[99]](#cite_note-99)

[George Williamson Crawford](/wiki/George_Williamson_Crawford "George Williamson Crawford"), a Yale Law School graduate, served as the city's first black [corporation counsel](/wiki/Corporation_counsel "Corporation counsel") from 1954 to 1962, under Mayor [Richard C. Lee](/wiki/Richard_C._Lee "Richard C. Lee").[[100]](#cite_note-100)

In 1970, the [New Haven Black Panther trials](/wiki/New_Haven_Black_Panther_trials "New Haven Black Panther trials") took place, the largest and longest trials in Connecticut history. [Black Panther Party](/wiki/Black_Panther_Party "Black Panther Party") co-founder [Bobby Seale](/wiki/Bobby_Seale "Bobby Seale") and ten other party members were tried for murdering an alleged informant. Beginning on May Day, the city became a center of protest for 12,000 Panther supporters, college students, and [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left") activists (including [Jean Genet](/wiki/Jean_Genet "Jean Genet"), [Benjamin Spock](/wiki/Benjamin_Spock "Benjamin Spock"), [Abbie Hoffman](/wiki/Abbie_Hoffman "Abbie Hoffman"), [Jerry Rubin](/wiki/Jerry_Rubin "Jerry Rubin"), and [John Froines](/wiki/John_Froines "John Froines")), who amassed on the [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), across the street from where the trials were being held. Violent confrontations between the demonstrators and the [New Haven Police](/wiki/New_Haven_Police_Department "New Haven Police Department") occurred, and several bombs were set off in the area by radicals. The event became a rallying point for the New Left and critics of the [Nixon Administration](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon").[[101]](#cite_note-101)[[102]](#cite_note-102)

During the summer of 2007, New Haven was the center of protests by [anti-immigration](/wiki/Anti-immigration "Anti-immigration") groups who opposed the city's program of offering municipal ID cards, known as the [Elm City Resident Card](/wiki/Elm_City_Resident_Card "Elm City Resident Card"), to [illegal immigrants](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States").[[103]](#cite_note-103)[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) In 2008, the country of [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador") opened a consulate in New Haven to serve the large Ecuadorean immigrant population in the area. It is the first foreign mission to open in New Haven since Italy opened a consulate (now closed) in the city in 1910.[[106]](#cite_note-106)[[107]](#cite_note-107)

In April 2009, the [United States Supreme Court](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court "United States Supreme Court") agreed to [hear a suit](/wiki/Ricci_v._DeStefano "Ricci v. DeStefano") over [reverse discrimination](/wiki/Reverse_discrimination "Reverse discrimination") brought by 20 white and Hispanic firefighters against the city. The suit involved the 2003 promotion test for the [New Haven Fire Department](/wiki/New_Haven_Fire_Department "New Haven Fire Department"). After the tests were scored, no [blacks](/wiki/Black_people "Black people") scored high enough to qualify for consideration for promotion, so the city announced that no one would be promoted. On June 29, 2009, the United States Supreme Court ruled in favor of the firefighters, agreeing that they were improperly denied promotion because of their race.[[108]](#cite_note-globe20090630-108) The case, *Ricci v. DeStefano*, became highly publicized and brought national attention to New Haven politics due to the involvement of then-[Supreme Court](/wiki/US_Supreme_Court "US Supreme Court") nominee (and Yale Law School graduate) [Sonia Sotomayor](/wiki/Sonia_Sotomayor "Sonia Sotomayor") in a lower court decision.[[109]](#cite_note-109)

[Garry Trudeau](/wiki/Garry_Trudeau "Garry Trudeau"), creator of the political *[Doonesbury](/wiki/Doonesbury "Doonesbury")* [comic strip](/wiki/Comic_strip "Comic strip"), attended Yale University. There he met fellow student and later [Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_the_United_States "Green Party of the United States") candidate for [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") [Charles Pillsbury](/wiki/Charles_Pillsbury_(attorney) "Charles Pillsbury (attorney)"), a long-time New Haven resident for whom Trudeau's comic strip is named. During his college years, Pillsbury was known by the nickname "The Doones". A [theory of international law](/wiki/International_legal_theory "International legal theory"), which argues for a sociological normative approach in regards to jurisprudence, is named the [New Haven Approach](/wiki/International_legal_theory#New_Haven_Approach "International legal theory"), after the city. Connecticut US senator [Richard Blumenthal](/wiki/Richard_Blumenthal "Richard Blumenthal") is a Yale graduate, as was former Connecticut US Senator [Joe Lieberman](/wiki/Joe_Lieberman "Joe Lieberman") who also was a New Haven resident for many years, before moving back to his hometown of [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford,_Connecticut "Stamford, Connecticut").[[110]](#cite_note-110)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Crime")]

Crime increased in the 1990s, with New Haven having one of the ten highest violent crime rates per capita in the United States.[[111]](#cite_note-111) In the late 1990s New Haven's crime began to stabilize. The city, adopting a policy of [community policing](/wiki/Neighborhood_watch "Neighborhood watch"), saw crime rates drop during the 2000s.[[112]](#cite_note-112)[[113]](#cite_note-113)

Violent crime levels vary dramatically among New Haven's neighborhoods, with some areas having crime rates in line with the state of Connecticut average, and others having extremely high rates of crime. A 2011 New Haven Health Department report identifies these issues in greater detail.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

In 2010, New Haven ranked as the 18th most dangerous city in the United States (albeit below the safety benchmark of 200.00 for the second consecutive year).[[115]](#cite_note-os.cqpress.com-115) However, according to a completely different analysis conducted by the "24/7 Wall Street Blog", in 2011 New Haven had risen to become the fourth most dangerous city in the United States, and was widely cited in the press as such.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117)

However, an analysis by the Regional Data Cooperative for Greater New Haven, Inc., has shown that due to issues of comparative denominators and other factors, such municipality-based rankings can be considered inaccurate.[[118]](#cite_note-118) For example, two cities of identical population can cover widely differing land areas, making such analyses irrelevant. The research organization called for comparisons based on neighborhoods, blocks, or standard methodologies (similar to those used by Brookings, DiversityData, and other established institutions), not based on municipalities.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Education")]

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

New Haven is a notable center for higher education. [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), at the heart of downtown, is one of the city's best known features and its largest employer.[[119]](#cite_note-119) New Haven is also home to [Southern Connecticut State University](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_State_University "Southern Connecticut State University"), part of the [Connecticut State University System](/wiki/Connecticut_State_University_System "Connecticut State University System"), and [Albertus Magnus College](/wiki/Albertus_Magnus_College "Albertus Magnus College"), a private institution. [Gateway Community College](/wiki/Gateway_Community_College "Gateway Community College") has a campus in downtown New Haven, formerly located in the [Long Wharf](/wiki/Long_Wharf_(New_Haven) "Long Wharf (New Haven)") district; Gateway consolidated into one campus downtown into a new state-of-the-art campus (on the site of the old Macy's building) and was open for the Fall 2012 semester.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)

There are several institutions immediately outside of New Haven, as well. [Quinnipiac University](/wiki/Quinnipiac_University "Quinnipiac University") and the [Paier College of Art](/wiki/Paier_College_of_Art "Paier College of Art") are located just to the north, in the town of [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"). The [University of New Haven](/wiki/University_of_New_Haven "University of New Haven") is located not in New Haven but in neighboring [West Haven](/wiki/West_Haven,_Connecticut "West Haven, Connecticut").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/d3/HGS_Student_Body_1911_12.jpg/250px-HGS_Student_Body_1911_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:HGS_Student_Body_1911_12.jpg)

The 1911 student body of the [Hopkins School](/wiki/Hopkins_School "Hopkins School"), the fifth-oldest educational institution in the United States

### Primary and secondary schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Primary and secondary schools")]

[New Haven Public Schools](/wiki/New_Haven_Public_Schools "New Haven Public Schools") is the school district serving the city. [Wilbur Cross High School](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_High_School "Wilbur Cross High School") and [Hillhouse High School](/wiki/Hillhouse_High_School "Hillhouse High School") are New Haven's two largest public secondary schools.

[Hopkins School](/wiki/Hopkins_School "Hopkins School"), a private school, was founded in 1660 and is the fifth-oldest educational institution in the United States.[[122]](#cite_note-122) New Haven is home to a number of other private schools as well as public magnet schools, including [Metropolitan Business Academy](/wiki/Metropolitan_Business_Academy "Metropolitan Business Academy"), High School in the Community, [Hill Regional Career High School](/wiki/Hill_Regional_Career_High_School "Hill Regional Career High School"), [Co-op High School](/wiki/Co-op_High_School "Co-op High School"), [New Haven Academy](/wiki/New_Haven_Academy "New Haven Academy"), [Edgewood Magnet School](/wiki/Edgewood_Magnet_School "Edgewood Magnet School"), [ACES Educational Center for the Arts](/wiki/ACES_Educational_Center_for_the_Arts "ACES Educational Center for the Arts"), the Foote School and the [Sound School](/wiki/Sound_School "Sound School"), all of which draw students from New Haven and suburban towns. New Haven is also home to two Achievement First charter schools, Amistad Academy and Elm City College Prep, and to Common Ground, an environmental charter school.

The city is renowned for its progressive school lunch programs,[[123]](#cite_note-123) and participation in statewide bussing efforts toward increased diversity in schools.[[124]](#cite_note-124)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[Livability.com](/wiki/Livability.com "Livability.com") named New Haven as the Best Foodie City in the country in 2014. There are dozens of [Zagat-rated](/wiki/Zagat_Survey "Zagat Survey") restaurants in New Haven, the most in Connecticut and the third most in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") (after [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston") and [Cambridge](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts")).[[125]](#cite_note-125) More than 120 restaurants are located within two blocks of the [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green").[[126]](#cite_note-126) The city is home to an eclectic mix of ethnic restaurants and small markets specializing in various foreign foods.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128) Represented cuisines include Malaysian, Ethiopian, Spanish, Belgian, French, Greek, Latin American, Mexican, Italian, Thai, Chinese, Japanese, Vietnamese, Korean, Indian, Jamaican, Cuban, Peruvian, Syrian/Lebanese, and Turkish.[[129]](#cite_note-129)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Frank_pepe_clam_pie.jpg/250px-Frank_pepe_clam_pie.jpg)](/wiki/File:Frank_pepe_clam_pie.jpg)

White clam pizza from [Pepe's](/wiki/Frank_Pepe_Pizzeria_Napoletana "Frank Pepe Pizzeria Napoletana"), in the classic [New Haven-style](/wiki/New_Haven-style_pizza "New Haven-style pizza")

New Haven's greatest culinary claim to fame may be its pizza, which has been claimed to be among the best in the country,[[130]](#cite_note-130)[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132)[[133]](#cite_note-133) or even in the world.[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135) [New Haven-style pizza](/wiki/New_Haven-style_pizza "New Haven-style pizza"), called "apizza", made its debut at the iconic [Frank Pepe Pizzeria Napoletana](/wiki/Frank_Pepe_Pizzeria_Napoletana "Frank Pepe Pizzeria Napoletana") (known as Pepe's) in 1925.[[136]](#cite_note-136) Apizza is baked in coal- or wood-fired brick ovens, and is notable for its thin crust. Apizza may be red (with a tomato-based sauce) or white (with a sauce of garlic and [olive oil](/wiki/Olive_oil "Olive oil")), and pies ordered "plain" are made without the otherwise customary [mozzarella](/wiki/Mozzarella "Mozzarella") (originally smoked mozzarella, known as "scamorza" in Italian). A white [clam](/wiki/Clam "Clam") pie is a well-known specialty of the restaurants on [Wooster Street](/wiki/Wooster_Square "Wooster Square") in the Little Italy section of New Haven, including Pepe's and [Sally's Apizza](/wiki/Sally%27s_Apizza "Sally's Apizza") (which opened in 1938). [Modern Apizza](/wiki/Modern_Apizza "Modern Apizza") on State Street, which opened in 1934, is also well-known.[[137]](#cite_note-137)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Louis-lunch.png/250px-Louis-lunch.png)](/wiki/File:Louis-lunch.png)

[Louis' Lunch](/wiki/Louis%27_Lunch "Louis' Lunch"), where the [hamburger](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger") was reputedly invented in 1900

A second New Haven [gastronomical](/wiki/Gastronomical "Gastronomical") claim to fame is [Louis' Lunch](/wiki/Louis%27_Lunch "Louis' Lunch"), which is located in a small brick building on Crown Street and has been serving fast food since 1895.[[138]](#cite_note-138) Though fiercely debated, the restaurant's founder Louis Lassen is credited by the [Library of Congress](/wiki/Library_of_Congress "Library of Congress") with inventing the [hamburger](/wiki/Hamburger "Hamburger") and [steak sandwich](/wiki/Steak_sandwich "Steak sandwich").[[139]](#cite_note-139)[[140]](#cite_note-140) Louis' Lunch [broils](/wiki/Broiling "Broiling") hamburgers, steak sandwiches and [hot dogs](/wiki/Hot_dogs "Hot dogs") vertically in original antique 1898 [cast iron](/wiki/Cast_iron "Cast iron") stoves using [gridirons](/wiki/Gridiron_(cooking) "Gridiron (cooking)"), patented by local resident Luigi Pieragostini in 1939, that hold the meat in place while it cooks.[[141]](#cite_note-141)

A third New Haven [gastronomical](/wiki/Gastronomical "Gastronomical") claim to fame is [Miya's](/wiki/Miya%27s "Miya's"), the first [sustainable sushi](/wiki/Sustainable_sushi "Sustainable sushi") restaurant in the world. Miya's, founded by Chef Yoshiko Lai in 1982, featured the first [sustainable seafood](/wiki/Sustainable_seafood "Sustainable seafood")-based sushi menu, the first plant-based sushi menu, and the first [invasive species](/wiki/Invasive_species "Invasive species") menu in the world. Second generation [Miya's](/wiki/Miya%27s "Miya's") chef, [Bun Lai](/wiki/Bun_Lai "Bun Lai"), is the 2016 [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") Champions of Change for Sustainable Seafood and a [James Beard Foundation](/wiki/James_Beard_Foundation "James Beard Foundation") Award nominee. Chef [Bun Lai](/wiki/Bun_Lai "Bun Lai") is credited as the first chef in the world for implementing a sustainability paradigm to the cuisine of sushi.[[142]](#cite_note-granata1-142)[[143]](#cite_note-143)[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145)[[146]](#cite_note-146)

During weekday lunchtime, over 150 lunch carts and food trucks cater to diners around the city.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The carts and food trucks cluster at four main points: on Long Wharf Drive, along the city's shoreline with quick access off Interstate 95,[[148]](#cite_note-148) by [Yale – New Haven Hospital](/wiki/Yale_%E2%80%93_New_Haven_Hospital "Yale – New Haven Hospital") in the center of the Hospital Green (Cedar and York streets), by Yale's [Trumbull College](/wiki/Trumbull_College "Trumbull College") (Elm and York streets), and on the intersection of Prospect and Sachem streets by the [Yale School of Management](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Management "Yale School of Management").[[149]](#cite_note-149)

Popular farmers' markets, managed by the local non-profit CitySeed,[[150]](#cite_note-150) set up shop weekly in several neighborhoods, including Westville/Edgewood Park, Fair Haven, Upper State Street, Wooster Square, and Downtown/New Haven Green.

### Theatre and film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Theatre and film")]

The city hosts numerous theatres and production houses, including the [Yale Repertory Theatre](/wiki/Yale_Repertory_Theatre "Yale Repertory Theatre"), the [Long Wharf Theatre](/wiki/Long_Wharf_Theatre "Long Wharf Theatre"), and the [Shubert Theatre](/wiki/Shubert_Theatre_(New_Haven) "Shubert Theatre (New Haven)"). There is also theatre activity from the [Yale School of Drama](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Drama "Yale School of Drama"), which works through the Yale University Theatre and the student-run [Yale Cabaret](/wiki/Yale_Cabaret "Yale Cabaret"). [Southern Connecticut State University](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_State_University "Southern Connecticut State University") hosts the Lyman Center for the Performing Arts. The shuttered Palace Theatre (opposite the Shubert Theatre) was renovated and reopened as the College Street Music Hall in May 2015. Smaller theatres include the Little Theater on Lincoln Street. Cooperative Arts and Humanities High School also has a theatre on College Street. The theatre is used for student productions, and is the home to weekly services to a local non-denominational church, the City Church New Haven.[[151]](#cite_note-151)

The Shubert Theatre once premiered many major theatrical productions before their Broadway debuts. Productions that premiered at the Shubert include *[Oklahoma!](/wiki/Oklahoma! "Oklahoma!")* (which was also written in New Haven[[152]](#cite_note-morganreed.com-152)), *[Carousel](/wiki/Carousel_(musical) "Carousel (musical)")*, *[South Pacific](/wiki/South_Pacific_(musical) "South Pacific (musical)")*, *[My Fair Lady](/wiki/My_Fair_Lady "My Fair Lady")*, *[The King and I](/wiki/The_King_and_I "The King and I")*, and *[The Sound of Music](/wiki/The_Sound_of_Music "The Sound of Music")*, and the [Tennessee Williams](/wiki/Tennessee_Williams "Tennessee Williams") play *[A Streetcar Named Desire](/wiki/A_Streetcar_Named_Desire_(play) "A Streetcar Named Desire (play)")*.

Bow Tie Cinemas owns and operates the Criterion Cinemas, the first new movie theater to open in New Haven in over 30 years and the first luxury movie complex in the city's history. The Criterion has seven screens and opened in November 2004, showing a mix of upscale first run commercial and independent film.[[153]](#cite_note-153)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Museums")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Yale_Peabody_Museum_2.JPG/250px-Yale_Peabody_Museum_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Yale_Peabody_Museum_2.JPG)

The historic [Peabody Museum of Natural History](/wiki/Peabody_Museum_of_Natural_History "Peabody Museum of Natural History") at Yale

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Yale-Center-for-British-Arts-New-Haven-Connecticut-04-2014d.jpg/250px-Yale-Center-for-British-Arts-New-Haven-Connecticut-04-2014d.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yale-Center-for-British-Arts-New-Haven-Connecticut-04-2014d.jpg)

The [Yale Center for British Art](/wiki/Yale_Center_for_British_Art "Yale Center for British Art"), designed by [Louis Kahn](/wiki/Louis_Kahn "Louis Kahn")

New Haven has a variety of museums, many of them associated with Yale.
