The [Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library](/wiki/Beinecke_Rare_Book_and_Manuscript_Library "Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library") features an original copy of the [Gutenberg Bible](/wiki/Gutenberg_Bible "Gutenberg Bible"). There is also the [Connecticut Children's Museum](/wiki/Connecticut_Children%27s_Museum "Connecticut Children's Museum"); the [Knights of Columbus](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus "Knights of Columbus") museum near that organization's world headquarters; the [Peabody Museum of Natural History](/wiki/Peabody_Museum_of_Natural_History "Peabody Museum of Natural History"); the [Yale University Collection of Musical Instruments](/wiki/Yale_University_Collection_of_Musical_Instruments "Yale University Collection of Musical Instruments"); the [Eli Whitney Museum](/wiki/Eli_Whitney_Museum "Eli Whitney Museum") (across the town line in [Hamden, Connecticut](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"), on Whitney Avenue); the [Yale Center for British Art](/wiki/Yale_Center_for_British_Art "Yale Center for British Art"), which houses the largest collection of British art outside the U.K.;[[154]](#cite_note-154) [Lost in New Haven](/wiki/Lost_in_New_Haven "Lost in New Haven"), and the [Yale University Art Gallery](/wiki/Yale_University_Art_Gallery "Yale University Art Gallery"), the western hemisphere's oldest college art museum.[[155]](#cite_note-155) New Haven is also home to the [New Haven Museum and Historical Society](/wiki/New_Haven_Museum_and_Historical_Society "New Haven Museum and Historical Society") on Whitney Avenue, which has a library of many primary source treasures dating from Colonial times to the present.

[Artspace](/wiki/Artspace,_New_Haven "Artspace, New Haven") on Orange Street is one of several contemporary art galleries around the city, showcasing the work of local, national, and international artists. Others include City Gallery and A. Leaf Gallery in the downtown area. Westville galleries include Kehler Liddell, Jennifer Jane Gallery, and The Hungry Eye. The [Erector Square](/wiki/Erector_Square "Erector Square") complex in the [Fair Haven](/wiki/Fair_Haven_(New_Haven) "Fair Haven (New Haven)") neighborhood houses, the Parachute Factory gallery along with numerous artist studios, and the complex serves as an active destination during City-Wide Open Studios held yearly in October.

New Haven is the home port of a life-size replica of the historical *[Freedom Schooner Amistad](/wiki/Amistad_(ship_replica) "Amistad (ship replica)")*, which is open for tours at Long Wharf pier at certain times during the summer. Also at Long Wharf pier is the *Quinnipiack* schooner, offering sailing cruises of the harbor area throughout the summer. The *Quinnipiack* also functions as a floating classroom for hundreds of local students.

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Music")]

The New Haven Green is the site of many free music concerts, especially during the summer months. These have included the [New Haven Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/New_Haven_Symphony_Orchestra "New Haven Symphony Orchestra"), the July Free Concerts on the Green, and the New Haven Jazz Festival in August. The Jazz Festival, which began in 1982, is one of the longest-running free outdoor festivals in the U.S., until it was canceled for 2007. Headliners such as [Dave Brubeck](/wiki/Dave_Brubeck "Dave Brubeck"), [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") and [Celia Cruz](/wiki/Celia_Cruz "Celia Cruz") have historically drawn 30,000 to 50,000 fans, filling up the New Haven Green to capacity. The New Haven Jazz Festival was revived in 2008 and has been sponsored since by Jazz Haven.[[156]](#cite_note-156)

New Haven is home to the concert venue [Toad's Place](/wiki/Toad%27s_Place "Toad's Place"), and a new venue, College Street Music Hall. The city has retained an alternative art and music underground that has helped to influence post-punk era music movements such as [indie](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock"), [college rock](/wiki/College_rock "College rock") and underground [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip-hop "Hip-hop").

The [Yale School of Music](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Music "Yale School of Music") contributes to the city's music scene by offering hundreds of free concerts throughout the year at venues in and around the Yale campus. Large performances are held in the 2,700-seat [Woolsey Hall](/wiki/Woolsey_Hall "Woolsey Hall") auditorium, which contains the [world's largest symphonic organs](/wiki/Newberry_Memorial_Organ "Newberry Memorial Organ"), while chamber music and recitals are performed in Sprague Hall.

[Hardcore](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music") band [Hatebreed](/wiki/Hatebreed "Hatebreed") are from [Wallingford](/wiki/Wallingford,_Connecticut "Wallingford, Connecticut"), but got their start in New Haven under the name [Jasta 14](/wiki/Jasta_14_(band) "Jasta 14 (band)"). The band [Miracle Legion](/wiki/Miracle_Legion "Miracle Legion") formed in New Haven in 1983. Folk musicians from New Haven include [Loren Mazzacane Connors](/wiki/Loren_Mazzacane_Connors "Loren Mazzacane Connors") and [Kath Bloom](/wiki/Kath_Bloom "Kath Bloom").

The Hillhouse Opera Company is a U.S. non-profit[[157]](#cite_note-157) opera company based in New Haven that performs in the New Haven area. Founded in 2008 by Victoria Leigh Gardner, Nicole Rodriguez and Jim Coatsworth Hillhouse Opera Company has performed operas as well as opera scenes programs, master classes and concert series.[[158]](#cite_note-158)[[159]](#cite_note-159)[[160]](#cite_note-160) In 2011, the Company professionally staged the works created through the Riverview Opera Project. The Riverview Opera Project created workshops for children and adolescents at Riverview Hospital, Connecticut's only state-funded psychiatric hospital for youth, and helped them to successfully create, produce, and perform four original operas.[[161]](#cite_note-161)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Festivals")]

In addition to the Jazz Festival (described above), New Haven serves as the home city of the annual [International Festival of Arts and Ideas](/wiki/International_Festival_of_Arts_and_Ideas "International Festival of Arts and Ideas"). New Haven's [Saint Patrick's Day](/wiki/Saint_Patrick%27s_Day "Saint Patrick's Day") parade, which began in 1842, is [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England")'s oldest and draws the largest crowds of any one-day spectator event in Connecticut.[[162]](#cite_note-162) The St. Andrew the Apostle Italian Festival has taken place in the historic [Wooster Square](/wiki/Wooster_Square "Wooster Square") neighborhood every year since 1900. Other parishes in the city celebrate the Feast of [Saint Anthony of Padua](/wiki/Saint_Anthony_of_Padua "Saint Anthony of Padua") and a carnival in honor of [St. Bernadette Soubirous](/wiki/St._Bernadette_Soubirous "St. Bernadette Soubirous").[[163]](#cite_note-163) New Haven celebrates [Powder House Day](/wiki/Powder_House_Day "Powder House Day") every April on the New Haven Green to commemorate the city's entrance into the [Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The annual Wooster Square Cherry Blossom Festival[[164]](#cite_note-164) commemorates the 1973 planting of 72 [Yoshino](/wiki/Prunus_%C3%97_yedoensis "Prunus × yedoensis") Japanese cherry blossom trees by the New Haven Historic Commission in collaboration with the New Haven Parks Department and residents of the neighborhood. The Festival now draws well over 5,000 visitors. The [Film Fest New Haven](/wiki/Film_Fest_New_Haven "Film Fest New Haven") has been held annually since 1995.

### Newspapers and media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Newspapers and media")]

New Haven is served by the daily *[New Haven Register](/wiki/New_Haven_Register "New Haven Register")*, the weekly "alternative" *[New Haven Advocate](/wiki/New_Haven_Advocate "New Haven Advocate")* (which is run by Tribune, the corporation owning the *[Hartford Courant](/wiki/Hartford_Courant "Hartford Courant")*), the online daily *New Haven Independent*,[[165]](#cite_note-165) and the monthly *Grand News Community Newspaper.* Downtown New Haven is covered by an in-depth civic news forum, [Design New Haven](/wiki/Design_New_Haven "Design New Haven"). The *Register* also backs *PLAY* magazine, a weekly entertainment publication. The city is also served by several student-run papers, including the *[Yale Daily News](/wiki/Yale_Daily_News "Yale Daily News")*, the weekly *[Yale Herald](/wiki/Yale_Herald "Yale Herald")* and a humor tabloid, *[Rumpus Magazine](/wiki/Rumpus_Magazine "Rumpus Magazine")*.

[WTNH](/wiki/WTNH "WTNH") Channel 8, the [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") affiliate for Connecticut, [WCTX](/wiki/WCTX "WCTX") Channel 59, the [MyNetworkTV](/wiki/MyNetworkTV "MyNetworkTV") affiliate for the state, [Connecticut Public Television](/wiki/Connecticut_Public_Television "Connecticut Public Television") station [WEDY](/wiki/WEDY "WEDY") channel 65, a [PBS](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") affiliate, and WTXX Channel 34, the IntrigueTV affiliate, broadcast from New Haven. All New York City news and sports team stations broadcast to New Haven County.

### Sports and athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Sports and athletics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg/250px-Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yale-Harvard-Game.jpg)

[Yale Bowl](/wiki/Yale_Bowl "Yale Bowl") during "The Game" in 2001

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Ingalls_Ice_Rink_%28Yale_Hockey%29.jpg/250px-Ingalls_Ice_Rink_%28Yale_Hockey%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ingalls_Ice_Rink_(Yale_Hockey).jpg)

[Ingalls Rink](/wiki/Ingalls_Rink "Ingalls Rink")

Main article: [Sports in New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/Sports_in_New_Haven,_Connecticut "Sports in New Haven, Connecticut")

New Haven has a history of professional sports franchises dating back to the 19th century[[166]](#cite_note-166) and has been the home to professional baseball, basketball, [football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), [hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey"), and soccer teams—including the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") from 1973 to 1974, who played at the [Yale Bowl](/wiki/Yale_Bowl "Yale Bowl"). Throughout the second half of the 20th century, New Haven consistently had minor league hockey and baseball teams, which played at the [New Haven Arena](/wiki/New_Haven_Arena "New Haven Arena") (built in 1926, demolished in 1972), [New Haven Coliseum](/wiki/New_Haven_Coliseum "New Haven Coliseum") (1972–2002), and [Yale Field](/wiki/Yale_Field "Yale Field") (1928–present).

When [John DeStefano, Jr.](/wiki/John_DeStefano,_Jr. "John DeStefano, Jr."), became mayor of New Haven in 1995, he outlined a plan to transform the city into a major cultural and arts center in the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), which involved investments in programs and projects other than sports franchises. As nearby [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport,_Connecticut "Bridgeport, Connecticut") built new sports facilities, the [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist "Brutalist") New Haven Coliseum rapidly deteriorated. Believing the upkeep on the venue to be a drain of tax dollars, the DeStefano administration closed the Coliseum in 2002; it was demolished in 2007. New Haven's last professional sports team, the [New Haven County Cutters](/wiki/New_Haven_County_Cutters "New Haven County Cutters"), left in 2009. The DeStefano administration did, however, see the construction of the New Haven Athletic Center in 1998, a 94,000-square-foot (8,700 m2) indoor athletic facility with a [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") of over 3,000. The NHAC, built adjacent to [Hillhouse High School](/wiki/Hillhouse_High_School "Hillhouse High School"), is used for New Haven public schools athletics, as well as large-scale area and state sporting events; it is the largest high school indoor sports complex in the state.[[167]](#cite_note-167)[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169)

New Haven was the host of the [1995 Special Olympics World Summer Games](/wiki/1995_Special_Olympics_World_Summer_Games "1995 Special Olympics World Summer Games"); then-President [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") spoke at the opening ceremonies.[[170]](#cite_note-170) The city is home to the [Pilot Pen International](/wiki/New_Haven_Open_at_Yale "New Haven Open at Yale") tennis event, which takes place every August at the [Connecticut Tennis Center](/wiki/Connecticut_Tennis_Center "Connecticut Tennis Center"), one of the largest tennis venues in the world.[[171]](#cite_note-171) New Haven biannually hosts "[The Game](/wiki/Harvard%E2%80%93Yale_football_rivalry "Harvard–Yale football rivalry")" between Yale and [Harvard](/wiki/Harvard_University "Harvard University"), the country's second-oldest college football rivalry. Numerous [road races](/wiki/Road_running "Road running") take place in New Haven, including the [USATF](/wiki/USATF "USATF") 20K Championship during the [New Haven Road Race](/wiki/New_Haven_Road_Race "New Haven Road Race").[[172]](#cite_note-172)

[Greater New Haven](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven") is home to a number of [college sports](/wiki/College_sports "College sports") teams. The [Yale Bulldogs](/wiki/Yale_Bulldogs "Yale Bulldogs") play Division I college sports, as do the [Quinnipiac Bobcats](/wiki/Quinnipiac_Bobcats "Quinnipiac Bobcats") in neighboring [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"). Division II athletics are played by [Southern Connecticut State University](/wiki/Southern_Connecticut_State_University "Southern Connecticut State University") and the [University of New Haven](/wiki/University_of_New_Haven "University of New Haven") (actually located in neighboring [West Haven](/wiki/West_Haven,_Connecticut "West Haven, Connecticut")), while [Albertus Magnus College](/wiki/Albertus_Magnus_College "Albertus Magnus College") athletes perform at the Division III level.

New Haven is home to many [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"), and [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") fans due to the proximity of New York City and Boston.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

[Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), deemed the "father of American football", was a New Havener.

The [New Haven Warriors](/wiki/New_Haven_Warriors "New Haven Warriors") [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") team play in the [AMNRL](/wiki/AMNRL "AMNRL"). They have a large number of [Pacific Islanders](/wiki/Pacific_Islander "Pacific Islander") playing for them.[[174]](#cite_note-174) Their field is located at the [West Haven High School](/wiki/West_Haven_High_School "West Haven High School")'s [Ken Strong Stadium](/wiki/Ken_Strong_Stadium "Ken Strong Stadium").[[175]](#cite_note-175) They won the 2008 AMNRL Grand Final.[[176]](#cite_note-176)

## Structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Structures")]

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Architecture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Yalenewhavenstructure1.jpg/250px-Yalenewhavenstructure1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yalenewhavenstructure1.jpg)

[Collegiate Gothic architecture](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic_architecture "Collegiate Gothic architecture") is popular in New Haven.

New Haven has many architectural landmarks dating from every important time period and architectural style in American history. The city has been home to a number of architects and architectural firms that have left their mark on the city including [Ithiel Town](/wiki/Ithiel_Town "Ithiel Town") and [Henry Austin](/wiki/Henry_Austin_(architect) "Henry Austin (architect)") in the 19th century and [Cesar Pelli](/wiki/Cesar_Pelli "Cesar Pelli"), [Warren Platner](/wiki/Warren_Platner "Warren Platner"), [Kevin Roche](/wiki/Kevin_Roche "Kevin Roche"), [Herbert Newman](/wiki/Herbert_Newman "Herbert Newman") and [Barry Svigals](/wiki/Barry_Svigals "Barry Svigals") in the 20th. The [Yale School of Architecture](/wiki/Yale_School_of_Architecture "Yale School of Architecture") has fostered this important component of the city's economy. [Cass Gilbert](/wiki/Cass_Gilbert "Cass Gilbert"), of the [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") school, designed New Haven's [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)") and the New Haven Free Public Library and was also commissioned for a [City Beautiful](/wiki/City_Beautiful "City Beautiful") plan in 1919. [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright"), [Marcel Breuer](/wiki/Marcel_Breuer "Marcel Breuer"), [Alexander Jackson Davis](/wiki/Alexander_Jackson_Davis "Alexander Jackson Davis"), [Philip C. Johnson](/wiki/Philip_Johnson "Philip Johnson"), [Gordon Bunshaft](/wiki/Gordon_Bunshaft "Gordon Bunshaft"), [Louis Kahn](/wiki/Louis_Kahn "Louis Kahn"), [James Gamble Rogers](/wiki/James_Gamble_Rogers "James Gamble Rogers"), [Frank Gehry](/wiki/Frank_Gehry "Frank Gehry"), [Charles Willard Moore](/wiki/Charles_Willard_Moore "Charles Willard Moore"), [Stefan Behnisch](/wiki/Behnisch_Architekten "Behnisch Architekten"), [James Polshek](/wiki/James_Polshek "James Polshek"), [Paul Rudolph](/wiki/Paul_Rudolph_(architect) "Paul Rudolph (architect)"), [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen") and [Robert Venturi](/wiki/Robert_Venturi "Robert Venturi") all have designed buildings in New Haven. Yale's 1950s-era [Ingalls Rink](/wiki/Ingalls_Rink "Ingalls Rink"), designed by Eero Saarinen, was included on the *[America's Favorite Architecture](/wiki/America%27s_Favorite_Architecture "America's Favorite Architecture")* list created in 2007.[[177]](#cite_note-177)

Several residential homes in New Haven were designed by Alice Washburn, a noted female architect whose Colonial Revival style set a standard for homes in the region.[[178]](#cite_note-178)

Many of the city's neighborhoods are well-preserved as walkable "museums" of 19th- and 20th-century American architecture, particularly by the [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), [Hillhouse Avenue](/wiki/Hillhouse_Avenue "Hillhouse Avenue") and other residential sections close to [Downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven"). Overall, a large proportion of the city's land area is National (NRHP) historic districts. One of the best sources on local architecture is *New Haven: Architecture and Urban Design*, by Elizabeth Mills Brown.[[179]](#cite_note-179)

See also: [List of tallest buildings in New Haven](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_Haven "List of tallest buildings in New Haven")

The five tallest buildings in New Haven are:[[180]](#cite_note-180)

1. [Connecticut Financial Center](/wiki/Connecticut_Financial_Center "Connecticut Financial Center") 383 ft (117m) 26 floors
2. [360 State Street](/wiki/360_State_Street "360 State Street") 338 ft (103m) 32 floors
3. [Knights of Columbus Building](/wiki/Knights_of_Columbus_Building_(New_Haven,_Connecticut) "Knights of Columbus Building (New Haven, Connecticut)") 321 ft (98m) 23 floors
4. [Kline Biology Tower](/wiki/Kline_Biology_Tower "Kline Biology Tower") 250 ft (76m) 16 floors
5. [Crown Towers](/wiki/Crown_Towers_(New_Haven,_Connecticut) "Crown Towers (New Haven, Connecticut)") 233 ft (71m) 22 floors

### Historic points of interest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Historic points of interest")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Crypt_beneath_Center_Church_on_the_Green%2C_New_Haven%2C_CT_%28October_2025%29.jpg/250px-Crypt_beneath_Center_Church_on_the_Green%2C_New_Haven%2C_CT_%28October_2025%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Crypt_beneath_Center_Church_on_the_Green,_New_Haven,_CT_(October_2025).jpg)

The Crypt - Center Church on the Green

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/NewHavenHillhouse5.JPG/250px-NewHavenHillhouse5.JPG)](/wiki/File:NewHavenHillhouse5.JPG)

The Graves-Dwight on Hillhouse Avenue

See also: [National Register of Historic Places listings in New Haven, Connecticut](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_New_Haven,_Connecticut "National Register of Historic Places listings in New Haven, Connecticut")

Many historical sites exist throughout the city, including [59 properties listed on the National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places_listings_in_New_Haven,_Connecticut "National Register of Historic Places listings in New Haven, Connecticut"). Of these, nine are among the [60 U.S. National Historic Landmarks in Connecticut](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_Connecticut "List of National Historic Landmarks in Connecticut"). The [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), one of the National Historic Landmarks, was formed in 1638, and is home to three 19th-century churches. Below the First Church of Christ in New Haven (referred to as the Center Church on the Green) lies a 17th-century crypt, which is open to visitors.[[181]](#cite_note-181) Some of the more famous burials include the first wife of [Benedict Arnold](/wiki/Benedict_Arnold "Benedict Arnold") and the aunt and grandmother of President [Rutherford B. Hayes](/wiki/Rutherford_B._Hayes "Rutherford B. Hayes"); Hayes visited the crypt while President in 1880.[[182]](#cite_note-182) The [Old Campus](/wiki/Old_Campus "Old Campus") of [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") is located next to the Green, and includes [Connecticut Hall](/wiki/Connecticut_Hall "Connecticut Hall"), Yale's oldest building and a National Historic Landmark. The [Hillhouse Avenue](/wiki/Hillhouse_Avenue "Hillhouse Avenue") area, which is listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") and is also a part of Yale's campus, has been called a walkable museum, due to its 19th-century mansions and street scape; [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") is said to have called Hillhouse Avenue "the most beautiful street in America" when visiting the city in 1868.[[183]](#cite_note-183)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Blackrockfort.jpg/250px-Blackrockfort.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blackrockfort.jpg)

The restored [Black Rock Fort](/wiki/Fort_Nathan_Hale "Fort Nathan Hale")

In 1660, [Edward Whalley](/wiki/Edward_Whalley "Edward Whalley") (a cousin and friend of [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell")) and [William Goffe](/wiki/William_Goffe "William Goffe"), two [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War") generals who signed the death warrant of [King Charles I](/wiki/King_Charles_I_of_England "King Charles I of England"), hid in a rock formation in New Haven after having fled England upon the [restoration](/wiki/Restoration_(England) "Restoration (England)") of [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") to the English throne.[[184]](#cite_note-184) They were later joined by a third [regicide](/wiki/Regicide "Regicide"), [John Dixwell](/wiki/John_Dixwell "John Dixwell"). The rock formation, which is now a part of [West Rock Park](/wiki/West_Rock_Park "West Rock Park"), is known as [Judges' Cave](/wiki/Judges%27_Cave "Judges' Cave"), and the path leading to the cave is called the [Regicides Trail](/wiki/Regicides_Trail "Regicides Trail").

After the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War") broke out in 1776, the [Connecticut colonial government](/wiki/Connecticut_Colony "Connecticut Colony") ordered the construction of [Black Rock Fort](/wiki/Fort_Nathan_Hale "Fort Nathan Hale") (to be built on top of an older 17th-century fort) to protect the port of New Haven. In 1779, during the [Battle of New Haven](/wiki/Tryon%27s_raid "Tryon's raid"), British soldiers captured Black Rock Fort and burned the barracks to the ground. The fort was reconstructed in 1807 by the federal government (on orders from the [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") administration), and rechristened [Fort Nathan Hale](/wiki/Fort_Nathan_Hale "Fort Nathan Hale"), after the [Revolutionary War hero](/wiki/Nathan_Hale "Nathan Hale") who had lived in New Haven. The cannons of Fort Nathan Hale were successful in defying British war ships during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). In 1863, during the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), a [second Fort Hale](/wiki/Fort_Nathan_Hale "Fort Nathan Hale") was built next to the original, complete with bomb-resistant bunkers and a moat, to defend the city should a [Southern raid](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") against New Haven be launched. The [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") deeded the site to the state in 1921, and all three versions of the fort have been restored. The site is now listed on the National Register of Historic Places and receives thousands of visitors each year.[[185]](#cite_note-185)[[186]](#cite_note-186)

[Grove Street Cemetery](/wiki/Grove_Street_Cemetery "Grove Street Cemetery"), a National Historic Landmark which lies adjacent to Yale's campus, contains the graves of [Roger Sherman](/wiki/Roger_Sherman "Roger Sherman"), [Eli Whitney](/wiki/Eli_Whitney "Eli Whitney"), [Noah Webster](/wiki/Noah_Webster "Noah Webster"), [Josiah Willard Gibbs](/wiki/Josiah_Willard_Gibbs "Josiah Willard Gibbs"), [Charles Goodyear](/wiki/Charles_Goodyear "Charles Goodyear") and [Walter Camp](/wiki/Walter_Camp "Walter Camp"), among other notable burials.[[187]](#cite_note-187) The cemetery is noted for its [Egyptian Revival](/wiki/Egyptian_Revival "Egyptian Revival") gateway, and is the oldest planned burial ground in the United States.[[188]](#cite_note-188) The [Union League Club](/wiki/Union_League "Union League") of New Haven building, located on Chapel Street, is notable for not only being a historic [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") building, but also is built on the site where Roger Sherman's home once stood; [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") is known to have stayed at the Sherman residence while President in 1789 (one of three times Washington visited New Haven throughout his lifetime).[[189]](#cite_note-189)[[190]](#cite_note-190)

Two sites pay homage to the time President and [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_United_States "Chief Justice of the United States") [William Howard Taft](/wiki/William_Howard_Taft "William Howard Taft") lived in the city, as both a student and later Professor at Yale: a plaque on Prospect Street marks the site where Taft's home formerly stood,[[191]](#cite_note-191) and downtown's Taft Apartment Building (formerly the Taft Hotel) bears the name of the former president who resided in the building for eight years before becoming Chief Justice of the United States.[[152]](#cite_note-morganreed.com-152)

Lighthouse Point Park, a public beach run by the city, was a popular tourist destination during the [Roaring Twenties](/wiki/Roaring_Twenties "Roaring Twenties"), attracting luminaries of the period such as [Babe Ruth](/wiki/Babe_Ruth "Babe Ruth") and [Ty Cobb](/wiki/Ty_Cobb "Ty Cobb").[[192]](#cite_note-192) The park remains popular among New Haveners, and is home to the [Five Mile Point Lighthouse](/wiki/Five_Mile_Point_Light "Five Mile Point Light"), constructed in 1847, and the Lighthouse Point [Carousel](/wiki/Carousel "Carousel"), constructed in 1916.[[193]](#cite_note-193)[[194]](#cite_note-194) Five Mile Point Light was decommissioned in 1877 following the construction of [Southwest Ledge Light](/wiki/Southwest_Ledge_Light "Southwest Ledge Light") at the entrance of the harbor, which remains in service to this day. Both of the lighthouses and the carousel are listed on the National Register of Historic Places.

Other historic sites in the city include the [Soldiers and Sailors Monument](/wiki/Soldiers_and_Sailors_Monument_(New_Haven) "Soldiers and Sailors Monument (New Haven)"), which stands at the summit of [East Rock](/wiki/East_Rock "East Rock"), the [Marsh Botanical Garden](/wiki/Marsh_Botanical_Garden "Marsh Botanical Garden"), [Wooster Square](/wiki/Wooster_Square "Wooster Square"), [Dwight Street](/wiki/Dwight_Street_Historic_District "Dwight Street Historic District"), [Louis' Lunch](/wiki/Louis%27_Lunch "Louis' Lunch"), and the [Farmington Canal](/wiki/Farmington_Canal "Farmington Canal"), all of which date back to the 19th century. Other historic parks besides the Green include [Edgerton Park](/wiki/Edgerton_(Hamden_and_New_Haven,_Connecticut) "Edgerton (Hamden and New Haven, Connecticut)"), [Edgewood Park](/wiki/Edgewood_Park,_New_Haven "Edgewood Park, New Haven"), and [East Rock Park](/wiki/East_Rock_Park "East Rock Park"), each of which is included on the National Register of Historic Places.

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transportation")]

### Rail

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Rail")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/New_Haven_Union_Station_from_Union_Avenue%2C_December_2016.JPG/250px-New_Haven_Union_Station_from_Union_Avenue%2C_December_2016.JPG)](/wiki/File:New_Haven_Union_Station_from_Union_Avenue,_December_2016.JPG)

[Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)") in 2016

New Haven has two railroad stations, connected to New York City and points along the Northeast Corridor by [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail"), [regional rail](/wiki/Regional_rail "Regional rail") and [inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail"). Service is provided by:

* [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad")'s [New Haven Line](/wiki/New_Haven_Line "New Haven Line") (commuter rail) to points west, such as [Bridgeport](/wiki/Bridgeport_station_(Connecticut) "Bridgeport station (Connecticut)"), [Stamford](/wiki/Stamford_Transportation_Center "Stamford Transportation Center"), [Greenwich](/wiki/Greenwich_station_(Metro-North) "Greenwich station (Metro-North)"), and [New York City](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal")
* [Shore Line East](/wiki/Shore_Line_East "Shore Line East") (commuter rail) to points east, such as [Old Saybrook](/wiki/Old_Saybrook_station "Old Saybrook station") and [New London](/wiki/New_London_Union_Station "New London Union Station"), with limited rush-hour service west to Stamford
* [Hartford Line](/wiki/Hartford_Line "Hartford Line") (commuter rail) to points north, such as [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden_Transit_Center "Meriden Transit Center"), [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford_Union_Station "Hartford Union Station"), [Windsor](/wiki/Windsor_station_(Connecticut) "Windsor station (Connecticut)"), and [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield_Union_Station_(Massachusetts) "Springfield Union Station (Massachusetts)")
* [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") (regional and intercity rail) to Northeast Corridor hubs, such as [New York](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station"), [Philadelphia](/wiki/30th_Street_Station "30th Street Station"), [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington_Union_Station "Washington Union Station"), and [Boston](/wiki/South_Station "South Station")

The city's main railroad station is the historic [Beaux-Arts](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture") [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)"), which serves Metro-North, Hartford Line, and Shore Line East commuter trains. Approximately 175 trains serve Union Station on weekdays. Union Station is also served by four Amtrak lines: the *[Northeast Regional](/wiki/Northeast_Regional "Northeast Regional")* and the [high-speed](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") *[Acela Express](/wiki/Acela_Express "Acela Express")* provide service to New York, Washington, D.C., and [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), and rank as the first and second busiest routes in the country; the [New Haven–Springfield Line](/wiki/New_Haven%E2%80%93Springfield_Line "New Haven–Springfield Line") provides service to [Hartford](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut") and [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"); and the *[Vermonter](/wiki/Vermonter_(train) "Vermonter (train)")* provides service to both Washington, D.C., and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont"), 15 miles (24 km) from the [Canada–US border](/wiki/Canada%E2%80%93United_States_border "Canada–United States border").

An additional station, [State Street Station](/wiki/State_Street_Station_(New_Haven) "State Street Station (New Haven)"), was opened in 2002, providing passengers easier access to [downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven"). State Street Station is currently serviced by Shore Line East and Hartford Line trains, plus some peak-hour Metro-North trips.

### Bus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Bus")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/CT_bus342face.JPG/250px-CT_bus342face.JPG)](/wiki/File:CT_bus342face.JPG)

A [New Haven Division](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit_New_Haven "Connecticut Transit New Haven") bus in [Downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven"), near the Green

The [New Haven Division](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit_New_Haven "Connecticut Transit New Haven") of [Connecticut Transit](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit "Connecticut Transit") (CT Transit), the state's bus system, is the second largest division in the state with 24 routes. All routes originate from the [New Haven Green](/wiki/New_Haven_Green "New Haven Green"), making it the central transfer hub of the city. Service is provided to 19 different municipalities throughout [Greater New Haven](/wiki/Greater_New_Haven "Greater New Haven"). Bus routes were formerly identified by letters, but as of October 8, 2017, all service was renamed using 200-series numbers, in accordance with a renumbering of CTtransit's statewide services.[[195]](#cite_note-195)

CT Transit's Union Station Shuttle provides free service from Union Station to the New Haven Green and several New Haven parking garages. [Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_Bus_Lines "Peter Pan Bus Lines") and [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") bus lines have scheduled stops at Union Station, and connections downtown can be made via the Union Station Shuttle. A private company operates the New Haven/Hartford Express which provides commuter bus service to Hartford. The Yale University Shuttle provides free transportation around New Haven for Yale students, faculty, and staff.

The New Haven Division buses follow routes that had originally been covered by [trolley](/wiki/Tram "Tram") service. [Horse-drawn streetcars](/wiki/Horsecar "Horsecar") began operating in New Haven in the 1860s, and by the mid-1890s all the lines had become electric. In the 1920s and 1930s, some of the trolley lines began to be replaced by bus lines, with the last trolley route converted to bus in 1948.[[196]](#cite_note-196) The City of New Haven is in the very early stages of considering the restoration of streetcar (light-rail) service, which has been absent since the postwar period.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)[[200]](#cite_note-200)

### Bicycle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Bicycle")]

#### Bikeshare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Bikeshare")]

On February 21, 2018, New Haven officially launched its **Bike New Haven** bikeshare program.[[201]](#cite_note-:0-201) based on dockless technology powered by Noa Technologies[[202]](#cite_note-202) At time of launch, the program features 10 docking stations and 100 bikes, spread throughout the urban core; there are plans to reach 30 bike stations and 300 bikes by the end of April 2018.[[201]](#cite_note-:0-201) The launch of the New Haven bikeshare program coincided with the launch of Yale University's own bikeshare program, which uses the same technology powered by Noa.[[203]](#cite_note-203)

#### Bike lanes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Bike lanes")]

In 2004, the first bike lane in the city was added.[[204]](#cite_note-204)[[205]](#cite_note-205) The city has added covered [bike parking](/wiki/Bicycle_parking "Bicycle parking") spots at Union Station, in order to facilitate bike commuting to the station.[[206]](#cite_note-206)

#### Farmington Canal Greenway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Farmington Canal Greenway")]

The [Farmington Canal Trail](/wiki/Farmington_Canal_Trail "Farmington Canal Trail") is a [rail trail](/wiki/Rail_trail "Rail trail") that will eventually run continuously from downtown New Haven to [Northampton, Massachusetts](/wiki/Northampton,_Massachusetts "Northampton, Massachusetts"). The scenic trail follows the path of the historic [New Haven and Northampton Company](/wiki/New_Haven_and_Northampton_Company "New Haven and Northampton Company") and the [Farmington Canal](/wiki/Farmington_Canal "Farmington Canal"). Currently, there is a continuous 14-mile (23 km) stretch of the trail from downtown, through [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut") and into [Cheshire](/wiki/Cheshire,_Connecticut "Cheshire, Connecticut"), making [bicycle commuting](/wiki/Bicycle_commuting "Bicycle commuting") between New Haven and those suburbs possible. The trail is part of the [East Coast Greenway](/wiki/East_Coast_Greenway "East Coast Greenway"), a proposed 3,000-mile (4,800 km) bike path that would link every major city on the [East Coast](/wiki/East_Coast_of_the_United_States "East Coast of the United States") from Florida to [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine").

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Roads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red%2C_White%2C_and_Blue_%2827460771747%29.jpg/250px-Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red%2C_White%2C_and_Blue_%2827460771747%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Q_Bridge_in_New_Haven_Illuminated_Red,_White,_and_Blue_(27460771747).jpg)

The [Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_Memorial_Bridge_(Connecticut) "Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge (Connecticut)"), locally known as the *Q Bridge*, carries ten lanes over the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River") along the [Connecticut Turnpike](/wiki/Connecticut_Turnpike "Connecticut Turnpike").

New Haven lies at the intersection of [Interstate 95](/wiki/Interstate_95_in_Connecticut "Interstate 95 in Connecticut") on the coast—which provides access southwards and/or westwards to the western coast of Connecticut and to New York City, and eastwards to the eastern Connecticut shoreline, [Rhode Island](/wiki/Rhode_Island "Rhode Island"), and eastern [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts")—and [Interstate 91](/wiki/Interstate_91#Connecticut "Interstate 91"), which leads northward to the interior of Connecticut, Massachusetts and [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont") and the Canada–US border. I-95 is notorious for traffic jams increasing with proximity to New York City; on the east side of New Haven it passes over the [Quinnipiac River](/wiki/Quinnipiac_River "Quinnipiac River") via the Pearl Harbor Memorial, or "[Q Bridge](/wiki/Pearl_Harbor_Memorial_Bridge_(Connecticut) "Pearl Harbor Memorial Bridge (Connecticut)")", which often presents a major bottleneck to traffic. I-91, however, is relatively less congested, except at the intersection with I-95 during peak travel times.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Oak Street Connector](/wiki/Oak_Street_Connector "Oak Street Connector") ([Connecticut Route 34](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_34 "Connecticut Route 34")) intersects I-91 at exit 1, just south of the I-95/I-91 interchange, and runs northwest for a few blocks as an expressway spur into downtown before emptying onto surface roads. The [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway") ([Connecticut Route 15](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_15 "Connecticut Route 15")) runs parallel to I-95 west of New Haven, turning northwards as it nears the city and then running northwards parallel to I-91 through the outer rim of New Haven and [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"), offering an alternative to the I-95/I-91 journey (restricted to non-commercial vehicles). Route 15 in New Haven is the site of the only highway tunnel in the state (officially designated as [Heroes Tunnel](/wiki/Heroes_Tunnel "Heroes Tunnel")), running through [West Rock](/wiki/West_Rock "West Rock"), home to [West Rock Park](/wiki/West_Rock_Park "West Rock Park") and the [Three Judges Cave](/wiki/Three_Judges_Cave "Three Judges Cave").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Heroes_Tunnel.jpg/250px-Heroes_Tunnel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heroes_Tunnel.jpg)

The [Wilbur Cross Parkway](/wiki/Wilbur_Cross_Parkway "Wilbur Cross Parkway") passes through [West Rock](/wiki/West_Rock "West Rock") via [Heroes Tunnel](/wiki/Heroes_Tunnel "Heroes Tunnel"), the only highway tunnel in Connecticut.

The city also has several major surface arteries. [U.S. Route 1](/wiki/U.S._Route_1_in_Connecticut "U.S. Route 1 in Connecticut") (Columbus Avenue, Union Avenue, Water Street, Forbes Avenue) runs in an east–west direction south of downtown serving [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(New_Haven) "Union Station (New Haven)") and leading out of the city to [Milford](/wiki/Milford,_Connecticut "Milford, Connecticut"), [West Haven](/wiki/West_Haven,_Connecticut "West Haven, Connecticut"), [East Haven](/wiki/East_Haven,_Connecticut "East Haven, Connecticut") and [Branford](/wiki/Branford,_Connecticut "Branford, Connecticut"). The main road from downtown heading northwest is Whalley Avenue (partly signed as [Route 10](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_10 "Connecticut Route 10") and [Route 63](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_63 "Connecticut Route 63")) leading to [Westville](/wiki/Westville_(New_Haven) "Westville (New Haven)") and [Woodbridge](/wiki/Woodbridge,_Connecticut "Woodbridge, Connecticut"). Heading north towards [Hamden](/wiki/Hamden,_Connecticut "Hamden, Connecticut"), there are two major thoroughfares, Dixwell Avenue and Whitney Avenue. To the northeast are Middletown Avenue ([Route 17](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_17 "Connecticut Route 17")), which leads to the Montowese section of North Haven, and Foxon Boulevard ([Route 80](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_80 "Connecticut Route 80")), which leads to the Foxon section of East Haven and to the town of [North Branford](/wiki/North_Branford,_Connecticut "North Branford, Connecticut"). To the west is [Route 34](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_34 "Connecticut Route 34"), which leads to the city of [Derby](/wiki/Derby,_Connecticut "Derby, Connecticut"). Other major intracity arteries are [Ella Grasso](/wiki/Ella_Grasso "Ella Grasso") Boulevard ([Route 10](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_10 "Connecticut Route 10")) west of downtown, and College Street, Temple Street, Church Street, Elm Street, and Grove Street in the downtown area.

Traffic safety is a major concern for drivers, pedestrians and cyclists in New Haven.[[207]](#cite_note-207) In addition to many traffic-related fatalities in the city each year, since 2005, over a dozen Yale students, staff and faculty have been killed or injured in traffic collisions on or near the campus.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

### Airport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Airport")]

[Tweed New Haven Airport](/wiki/Tweed_New_Haven_Airport "Tweed New Haven Airport") is located within the city limits 3 miles (5 km) east of the business district, straddling the border with neighboring East Haven. Service to approximately 25 cities is provided by [Avelo Airlines](/wiki/Avelo_Airlines "Avelo Airlines"), which established its first East Coast base at the airport in 2021. [Breeze Airways](/wiki/Breeze_Airways "Breeze Airways") plans to begin operations at Tweed in December 2024, and will provide service to 10 destinations. The airport is currently planning a runway lengthening and new terminal on the East Haven side of the airport.

Bus service between [Downtown New Haven](/wiki/Downtown_New_Haven "Downtown New Haven") and Tweed is available via the [CT Transit New Haven Division](/wiki/Connecticut_Transit_New_Haven "Connecticut Transit New Haven").

### Seaport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Seaport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/60/Newhaven_harbor_pan.png/250px-Newhaven_harbor_pan.png)](/wiki/File:Newhaven_harbor_pan.png)

Port of New Haven

[New Haven Harbor](/wiki/New_Haven_Harbor "New Haven Harbor") is home to the Port of New Haven, a deep-water seaport with three berths capable of hosting vessels and barges as well as the facilities required to handle [break bulk cargo](/wiki/Break_bulk_cargo "Break bulk cargo"). The port has the capacity to load 200 trucks a day from the ground or via loading docks. [Providence and Worcester Railroad](/wiki/Providence_and_Worcester_Railroad "Providence and Worcester Railroad") provides freight rail transportation to the port, with the railroad operating a switch engine for yard movements, a private siding for loading and unloading, and trackage over the [Tomlinson Lift Bridge](/wiki/Tomlinson_Lift_Bridge "Tomlinson Lift Bridge") toward the Northeast Corridor. Approximately 400,000 square feet (40,000 m2) of inside storage and 50 acres (200,000 m2) of outside storage are available at the site. Five shore cranes with a 250-ton capacity and 26 forklifts, each with a 26-ton capacity, are also available.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On June 17, 2013, the city commissioned the [*Nathan Hale*](/wiki/Nathan_Hale_(fireboat) "Nathan Hale (fireboat)"), a 36 foot (11 m) port security vessel capable of serving [search and rescue](/wiki/Search_and_rescue "Search and rescue"), firefighting, and constabulary roles.[[209]](#cite_note-NewHavenRegister2013-06-17-209)[[210]](#cite_note-Nhi2014-06-17-210)

## Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Sterling_Memorial_Library_Interior_%28bright_color_scale%29.jpg/250px-Sterling_Memorial_Library_Interior_%28bright_color_scale%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sterling_Memorial_Library_Interior_(bright_color_scale).jpg)

Yale's [Sterling Memorial Library](/wiki/Sterling_Memorial_Library "Sterling Memorial Library")

### Hospitals and medicine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Hospitals and medicine")]

The New Haven area supports several medical facilities that are considered some of the best hospitals in the country. There are two major medical centers downtown: [Yale – New Haven Hospital](/wiki/Yale_%E2%80%93_New_Haven_Hospital "Yale – New Haven Hospital") has four pavilions, including the Yale – New Haven Children's Hospital[[211]](#cite_note-211) and the Smilow Cancer Hospital;[[212]](#cite_note-212) the [Hospital of Saint Raphael](/wiki/Hospital_of_Saint_Raphael "Hospital of Saint Raphael") is several blocks north, and touts its excellent cardiac emergency care program. Smaller downtown health facilities are the Temple Medical Center located downtown on Temple Street, Connecticut Mental Health Center/[[213]](#cite_note-213) across Park Street from Y-NHH, and the Hill Health Center,[[214]](#cite_note-214) which serves the working-class Hill neighborhood. A large Veterans Affairs hospital is located in neighboring West Haven. To the west in [Milford](/wiki/Milford,_Connecticut "Milford, Connecticut") is [Milford Hospital](/wiki/Milford_Hospital_(Connecticut) "Milford Hospital (Connecticut)"), and to the north in [Meriden](/wiki/Meriden,_Connecticut "Meriden, Connecticut") is the MidState Medical Center.[[215]](#cite_note-215)

In recent years, Yale and the City of New Haven have been working to build a medical and biotechnology research hub in the city and Greater New Haven region.[[216]](#cite_note-216) The city, state and Yale together run Science Park,[[217]](#cite_note-217) a large site three blocks northwest of Yale's [Science Hill](/wiki/Science_Hill_(Yale_University) "Science Hill (Yale University)") campus.[[218]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-218) This multi-block site, approximately bordered by Mansfield Street, Division Street, and Shelton Avenue, is the former home of [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester_Repeating_Arms_Company "Winchester Repeating Arms Company")'s and Olin Corporation's 45 large-scale factory buildings. Currently, sections of the site are large-scale parking lots or abandoned structures, but there is also a large remodeled and functioning area of buildings (leased primarily by a private developer) with numerous Yale employees, financial service and biotech companies.

A second biotechnology district is being planned for the median strip on Frontage Road, on land cleared for the never-built [Route 34](/wiki/Connecticut_Route_34 "Connecticut Route 34") extension.[[218]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-218) As of late 2009, a [Pfizer](/wiki/Pfizer "Pfizer") drug-testing clinic, a medical laboratory building serving Yale – New Haven Hospital, and a mixed-use structure containing parking, housing and office space, have been constructed on this corridor.[[218]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-218) A former [SNET](/wiki/Southern_New_England_Telephone "Southern New England Telephone") telephone building at 300 George Street is being converted into lab space, and has been quite successful so far in attracting biotechnology and medical firms.[[218]](#cite_note-autogenerated5-218)

### Power supply facilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Power supply facilities")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/English_Station_Power_Plant.jpg/250px-English_Station_Power_Plant.jpg)](/wiki/File:English_Station_Power_Plant.jpg)

[English Station](/wiki/English_Station "English Station"), an abandoned [thermal power plant](/wiki/Thermal_power_plant "Thermal power plant") in downtown

Electricity for New Haven is generated by a 448 MW oil and gas-fired generating station located on the shore at New Haven Harbor.[[219]](#cite_note-219) [PPL Corporation](/wiki/PPL_Corporation "PPL Corporation") operates a 220 MW peaking natural gas turbine plant in nearby Wallingford.

Near New Haven there is the [static inverter plant](/wiki/Static_inverter_plant "Static inverter plant") of the [HVDC](/wiki/HVDC "HVDC") [Cross Sound Cable](/wiki/Cross_Sound_Cable "Cross Sound Cable").

There are three [PureCell](/wiki/PureCell_System "PureCell System") Model 400 [fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell") placed in the city of New Haven—one at the [New Haven Public Schools](/wiki/New_Haven_Public_Schools "New Haven Public Schools") and newly constructed Roberto Clemente School,[[220]](#cite_note-220) one at the mixed-use [360 State Street](/wiki/360_State_Street "360 State Street") building,[[221]](#cite_note-221) and one at [City Hall](/wiki/New_Haven_City_Hall_and_County_Courthouse "New Haven City Hall and County Courthouse").[[222]](#cite_note-222) According to Giovanni Zinn of the city's Office of Sustainability, each fuel cell may save the city up to $1 million in energy costs over a decade.[[223]](#cite_note-223) The fuel cells were provided by [ClearEdge Power](/wiki/ClearEdge_Power "ClearEdge Power"),[[224]](#cite_note-224) formerly [UTC Power](/wiki/UTC_Power "UTC Power").[[225]](#cite_note-225) Additional fuel cells are located at the [Yale Peabody Museum of Natural History](/wiki/Peabody_Museum_of_Natural_History "Peabody Museum of Natural History") and at the Greater New Haven Water Pollution Control Authority (GNHWPCA).[[226]](#cite_note-226) [Ikea](/wiki/IKEA "IKEA")'s New Haven facility also utilizes a 250 kW fuel cell and 940.8 kW solar array.[[227]](#cite_note-227)

New Haven recently installed solar panels at 11 city schools with a combined power generation capacity of 1.8 MW.[[228]](#cite_note-228) Owned and maintained by Greenskies, the panels allow New Haven to purchase electricity at a discounted rate through a power-purchasing agreement. The panels bring New Haven's solar capacity to 2.8 MW and will help New Haven meet its commitment to powering 100% of its municipal operations through clean energy, which it made in Summer 2017[[229]](#cite_note-229) and reaffirmed in the 2018 New Haven Climate and Sustainability Framework.[[230]](#cite_note-230)

Several movies have been filmed in New Haven since 2000, including *[Mona Lisa Smile](/wiki/Mona_Lisa_Smile "Mona Lisa Smile")* (2003), with [Julia Roberts](/wiki/Julia_Roberts "Julia Roberts"),[[231]](#cite_note-231) *[The Life Before Her Eyes](/wiki/The_Life_Before_Her_Eyes "The Life Before Her Eyes")* (2007), with [Uma Thurman](/wiki/Uma_Thurman "Uma Thurman"), and *[Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull](/wiki/Indiana_Jones_and_the_Kingdom_of_the_Crystal_Skull "Indiana Jones and the Kingdom of the Crystal Skull")* (2008) directed by [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") and starring [Harrison Ford](/wiki/Harrison_Ford "Harrison Ford"), [Cate Blanchett](/wiki/Cate_Blanchett "Cate Blanchett") and [Shia LaBeouf](/wiki/Shia_LaBeouf "Shia LaBeouf").[[232]](#cite_note-232) The filming of *Crystal Skull* involved an extensive chase sequence through the streets of New Haven. Several downtown streets were closed to traffic and received a "makeover" to look like streets of 1957, when the film is set. 500 locals were cast as extras for the film.[[233]](#cite_note-233)[[234]](#cite_note-234) In *[Everybody's Fine](/wiki/Everybody%27s_Fine_(2009_film) "Everybody's Fine (2009 film)")* (2009), [Robert De Niro](/wiki/Robert_De_Niro "Robert De Niro") has a close encounter in what is supposed to be the [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver") train station; the scene was filmed in New Haven's Union Station.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/New_Haven_Union_Station_tunnels.jpg/250px-New_Haven_Union_Station_tunnels.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_Haven_Union_Station_tunnels.jpg)

Union Station tunnel as seen in *[Everybody's Fine](/wiki/Everybody%27s_Fine_(2009_film) "Everybody's Fine (2009 film)")* (2009)

New Haven is mentioned in the song [Peace Frog](/wiki/Peace_Frog "Peace Frog") by [the Doors](/wiki/The_Doors "The Doors"), referencing a 1967 incident where vocalist [Jim Morrison](/wiki/Jim_Morrison "Jim Morrison") was arrested for "attempting to incite a riot" in the middle of a concert at the [New Haven Arena](/wiki/New_Haven_Arena "New Haven Arena").

## Sister cities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=49 "Edit section: Sister cities")]

New Haven's [sister cities](/wiki/Sister_city "Sister city") are:[[235]](#cite_note-235)

* [![Israel](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Flag_of_Israel.svg/40px-Flag_of_Israel.svg.png)](/wiki/Israel "Israel") [Afula](/wiki/Afula "Afula"), Israel
* [![Italy](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/03/Flag_of_Italy.svg/40px-Flag_of_Italy.svg.png)](/wiki/Italy "Italy") [Amalfi](/wiki/Amalfi "Amalfi"), Italy
* [![France](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/c/c3/Flag_of_France.svg/40px-Flag_of_France.svg.png)](/wiki/France "France") [Avignon](/wiki/Avignon "Avignon"), France
* [![China](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/China "China") [Changsha](/wiki/Changsha "Changsha"), China
* [![Sierra Leone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Flag_of_Sierra_Leone.svg/40px-Flag_of_Sierra_Leone.svg.png)](/wiki/Sierra_Leone "Sierra Leone") [Freetown](/wiki/Freetown "Freetown"), Sierra Leone
* [![Vietnam](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Flag_of_Vietnam.svg/40px-Flag_of_Vietnam.svg.png)](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") [Huế](/wiki/Hu%E1%BA%BF "Huế"), Vietnam
* [![Nicaragua](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Flag_of_Nicaragua.svg/40px-Flag_of_Nicaragua.svg.png)](/wiki/Nicaragua "Nicaragua") [León, Nicaragua](/wiki/Le%C3%B3n,_Nicaragua "León, Nicaragua"), Nicaragua
* [![Taiwan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Flag_of_the_Republic_of_China.svg/40px-Flag_of_the_Republic_of_China.svg.png)](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") [Taichung](/wiki/Taichung "Taichung"), Taiwan
* [![Mexico](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fc/Flag_of_Mexico.svg/40px-Flag_of_Mexico.svg.png)](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") [San Francisco Tetlanohcan](/wiki/San_Francisco_Tetlanohcan_Municipality "San Francisco Tetlanohcan Municipality"), Mexico

Some of these were selected because of historical connection—Freetown because of the *Amistad* trial. Others, such as Amalfi and Afula, reflect ethnic groups in New Haven.

In 1990, the United Nations named New Haven a "[Peace Messenger City](/wiki/United_Nations_Peace_Messenger_Cities "United Nations Peace Messenger Cities")".

## Further reading

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_Haven,_Connecticut&action=edit&section=52 "Edit section: Further reading")]

* Leonard Bacon, *Thirteen Historical Discourses* (New Haven, 1839)
* C. H. Hoadley (editor), *Records of the Colony of New Haven, 1638–1665* (two volumes, Hartford, 1857–58)
* J. W. Barber, *History and Antiquities of New Haven* (third edition, New Haven, 1870)
* C. H. Levermore, *Town and City Government of New Haven* (Baltimore, 1886)
* C. H. Levermore, *Republic of New Haven: A History of Municipal Evolution* (Baltimore, 1886)
* E. S. Bartlett, *Historical Sketches of New Haven* (New Haven, 1897)
* F. H. Cogswell, "New Haven" in L. P. Powell (editor), *Historic Towns of New England* (New York, 1898)
* H. T. Blake, *Chronicles of New Haven Green* (New Haven, 1898)
* E. E. Atwater, *History of the Colony of New Haven* (New edition, New Haven, 1902)
* ["New Haven"](https://archive.org/stream/handbookofneweng00sargiala#page/94/mode/2up), *Handbook of New England*, Boston: [Porter E. Sargent](/wiki/Porter_Sargent "Porter Sargent"), 1916, [OCLC](/wiki/OCLC_(identifier) "OCLC (identifier)") [16726464](https://search.worldcat.org/oclc/16726464)
* [Robert A. Dahl](/wiki/Robert_A._Dahl "Robert A. Dahl"), *[Who Governs? Democracy and Power in An American City](/wiki/Who_Governs%3F "Who Governs?")* (Yale University Press, New Haven, 1961)
* [William Lee Miller](/wiki/William_Lee_Miller "William Lee Miller"), *The Fifteenth Ward and the Great Society* (Houghton Mifflin/Riverside, 1966)
* Douglas W. Rae, *City: Urbanism and Its End* (New Haven, 2003)
* *New Haven City Yearbooks*
* Michael Sletcher, *New Haven: From Puritanism to the Age of Terrorism* (Charleston, 2004)
* Preston C. Maynard and Majorey B. Noyes, (editors), "Carriages and Clocks, Corsets and Locks: the Rise and Fall of an Industrial City—New Haven, Connecticut" (University Press of New England, 2005)
* Mandi Isaacs Jackson, *Model City Blues: Urban Space and Organized Resistance in New Haven* (Temple University Press, 2008)
* James Cersonsky, "Whose New Haven? Reversing the Slant of the Knowledge Economy" (*Dissent*, February 15, 2011)
* Paul Bass, "New Hope for New Haven, Connecticut" (*Nation*, January 25, 2012)
